abstract:
We introduce UniToken, an auto-regressive generation model that encodes
visual inputs through a combination of discrete and continuous representations,
enabling seamless integration of unified visual understanding and image
generation tasks. Unlike previous approaches that rely on unilateral visual
representations, our unified visual encoding framework captures both high-level
semantics and low-level details, delivering multidimensional information that
empowers heterogeneous tasks to selectively assimilate domain-specific
knowledge based on their inherent characteristics. Through in-depth
experiments, we uncover key principles for developing a unified model capable
of both visual understanding and image generation. Extensive evaluations across
a diverse range of prominent benchmarks demonstrate that UniToken achieves
state-of-the-art performance, surpassing existing approaches. These results
establish UniToken as a robust foundation for future research in this domain.
The code and models are available at this https URL

keyInsights: Unified discrete-continuous visual encoding effectively mitigates task interference
Balanced 1:1 ratio between understanding and generation tasks is optimal for training
Scaling up resolution and fine-tuning visual transformers significantly improves comprehension
originalProblem: Existing unified models either suffer from information loss due to discrete-only encoding or increased complexity from decoupled visual encoders
Task interference between visual understanding and generation tasks limits model performance
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on visual understanding benchmarks
Demonstrates competitive performance on image generation tasks comparable to other autoregressive models
Successfully balances both understanding and generation capabilities within a single architecture
Reduces information loss while maintaining architectural simplicity
solution: Dual visual encoder combining SigLIP for continuous semantic features and VQ-Tokenizer for discrete tokens
Three-stage training pipeline with balanced dataset distribution and advanced resolution scaling techniques
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University and Meituan develop UniToken, a unified multimodal model that harmonizes visual understanding and generation through a novel dual visual encoder combining continuous semantic features and discrete tokens, achieving competitive performance across multiple benchmarks while addressing the limitations of discrete-only and decoupled encoding approaches.

title: UniToken: Harmonizing Multimodal Understanding and Generation through  Unified Visual Encoding

abstract: We present NextFlow, a unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer trained on 6 trillion interleaved text-image discrete tokens. By leveraging a unified vision representation within a unified autoregressive architecture, NextFlow natively activates multimodal understanding and generation capabilities, unlocking abilities of image editing, interleaved content and video generation. Motivated by the distinct nature of modalities - where text is strictly sequential and images are inherently hierarchical - we retain next-token prediction for text but adopt next-scale prediction for visual generation. This departs from traditional raster-scan methods, enabling the generation of 1024x1024 images in just 5 seconds - orders of magnitude faster than comparable AR models. We address the instabilities of multi-scale generation through a robust training recipe. Furthermore, we introduce a prefix-tuning strategy for reinforcement learning. Experiments demonstrate that NextFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance among unified models and rivals specialized diffusion baselines in visual quality.

keyInsights: A single, computationally efficient autoregressive model can rival or surpass specialized diffusion and hybrid models in both high-fidelity visual generation and complex multimodal understanding.

Next-scale prediction, combined with a robust training pipeline, effectively overcomes the historical efficiency bottleneck of autoregressive visual generation, making it practical for high-resolution output.
Dual-codebook tokenization and scale-aware reweighting are crucial for bridging the semantic gap in visual representations, enabling advanced reasoning and coherent multimodal interactions.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI often compartmentalizes Large Language Models (LLMs) for reasoning and Diffusion Models for generation, creating friction and limiting true integration.
Hybrid autoregressive-diffusion architectures suffer from re-encoding overheads and a gap between generation and understanding due to separate representations.

Previous pure autoregressive models faced computational inefficiency (slow high-resolution visual generation) and a semantic gap (visual tokens optimized for pixel fidelity, lacking high-level meaning).
results: Generates 1024x1024 images in 5 seconds, requiring approximately 6x fewer FLOPs than MMDiT-based diffusion models at the same resolution.

Achieved state-of-the-art in image generation (DPG 88.32, GenEval 0.84) and image editing (ImgEdit 4.49, OmniContext SC 9.22, GEdit-Bench 7.87, EditCanvas 8.04), outperforming strong baselines like GPT-4o and Emu3.5 in various metrics.
Demonstrates native Chain-of-Thought reasoning (+0.1 boost on WISE benchmark) and in-context learning, and competitive multimodal understanding on several benchmarks with a 7B parameter model.
solution: A unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer, initialized from Qwen2.5-VL-7B, handles both multimodal understanding and generation with a single prediction head.
Introduces 'next-scale prediction' for hierarchical visual generation, moving from coarse to fine details to drastically improve efficiency over raster-scan methods.
Employs a dual-codebook tokenizer to decouple semantic and pixel-level feature learning, ensuring both high-level conceptual alignment and fine-grained visual fidelity.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at ByteDance developed NextFlow, a unified autoregressive transformer model that integrates multimodal understanding and generation. It achieves leading performance in image generation, editing, and reasoning tasks, notably generating 1024x1024 images in 5 seconds while demonstrating state-of-the-art prompt following and editing capabilities.

title: NextFlow: Unified Sequential Modeling Activates Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract:
Current image generation and editing methods primarily process textual
prompts as direct inputs without reasoning about visual composition and
explicit operations. We present Generation Chain-of-Thought (GoT), a novel
paradigm that enables generation and editing through an explicit language
reasoning process before outputting images. This approach transforms
conventional text-to-image generation and editing into a reasoning-guided
framework that analyzes semantic relationships and spatial arrangements. We
define the formulation of GoT and construct large-scale GoT datasets containing
over 9M samples with detailed reasoning chains capturing semantic-spatial
relationships. To leverage the advantages of GoT, we implement a unified
framework that integrates Qwen2.5-VL for reasoning chain generation with an
end-to-end diffusion model enhanced by our novel Semantic-Spatial Guidance
Module. Experiments show our GoT framework achieves excellent performance on
both generation and editing tasks, with significant improvements over
baselines. Additionally, our approach enables interactive visual generation,
allowing users to explicitly modify reasoning steps for precise image
adjustments. GoT pioneers a new direction for reasoning-driven visual
generation and editing, producing images that better align with human intent.
To facilitate future research, we make our datasets, code, and pretrained
models publicly available at this https URL

author_info.institution: Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

keyInsights: Integrating explicit reasoning chains improves coherence and controllability of generated images
Combining semantic and spatial guidance enables precise control over object layout and relationships
Interactive modification of reasoning steps allows for refined image adjustments
originalProblem: Current image generation methods lack explicit reasoning about visual composition and spatial relationships
Existing approaches treat planning and generation as separate stages without true integration of language reasoning
Generated images often lack coherence or fail to accurately reflect human intent
results: Achieved highest overall score on GenEval benchmark for text-to-image generation
Outperformed baselines on CLIP-I and CLIP-T metrics for Emu-Edit benchmark
Demonstrated successful interactive control through modification of reasoning chains
Reduced computational costs while maintaining or improving generation quality
solution: Developed Generation Chain-of-Thought (GoT) paradigm that outputs step-by-step reasoning before image generation
Created large-scale datasets (8.4M images for generation, 920K for editing) with detailed reasoning chains
Implemented semantic-spatial guidance module to condition diffusion process based on reasoning steps
paper_summary.summary: A unified framework integrates reasoning capabilities into visual generation and editing by combining multimodal language models with a semantic-spatial guidance module, demonstrating superior performance on GenEval and Emu-Edit benchmarks while enabling interactive control through explicit reasoning chains.

title: GoT: Unleashing Reasoning Capability of Multimodal Large Language Model  for Visual Generation and Editing

abstract: Unified multimodal generative models aim to integrate image understanding and generation abilities, offering significant advantages in harnessing multimodal corpora, particularly interleaved text-image data. However, existing unified models exhibit limitations in image synthesis quality, autoregressive error accumulation, and image editing capability. In this work, we propose Nexus-Gen, a novel architecture that unifies image understanding, generation, and editing tasks in a shared image embedding space. This shared space serves as a bridge for the autoregressive and diffusion models, which seamlessly integrates their complementary strengths in cross-modal modeling. To mitigate the severe error accumulation during autoregressive embedding prediction, we propose a novel prefilled autoregression strategy that aligns training-inference dynamics by prefilling input sequences with learnable embeddings. After multi-stage and multi-task training on our constructed large-scale dataset with 26.3 million samples, Nexus-Gen achieves state-of-the-art performance on the evaluation benchmarks spanning image understanding, generation and editing tasks. All models, datasets, and source codes are released in this https URL to facilitate further advancements across the field.

keyInsights: The "prefilled autoregression" strategy effectively mitigates error accumulation during the autoregressive prediction of continuous embeddings, resulting in significantly lower MSE loss compared to naive autoregression.
A specialized dual-conditioning editing decoder is critical for high-fidelity image editing, substantially improving the preservation of non-edited regions compared to a general generation decoder.

An optimal token quantity (81 tokens) for autoregressive generation balances semantic coverage and ease of prediction, while a higher token count (324 tokens) from the input image is beneficial for detail preservation in editing tasks.
originalProblem: Unified multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often achieve lower image synthesis quality compared to specialized diffusion models.
Autoregressive generation of continuous image embeddings in MLLMs using diffusion decoders suffers from severe error accumulation, leading to degraded image quality.
Many existing unified models lack robust and validated capabilities for complex downstream tasks, particularly high-fidelity fine-grained image editing.
results: Achieves leading scores across multimodal understanding benchmarks (MME, SEED-Bench, MMMU, TextVQA) with its 7B autoregressive component, without additional parameters or performance loss.

Attains a state-of-the-art overall score of 0.81 on the GenEval benchmark for image generation after instruction tuning, showing particular strength in compositional understanding (e.g., "Two Object," "Counting").
Demonstrates superior performance on the ImagePulse benchmark for image editing, with a CLIP-T score of 0.324 and strong fidelity preservation (L1 0.134, CLIP-O 0.909, DINO-O 0.834).
solution: Nexus-Gen proposes a unified architecture integrating a vision encoder (from Qwen2.5-VL), a 7B autoregressive model, and a diffusion-based vision decoder operating within a shared continuous image embedding space.

It introduces a novel "prefilled autoregression" strategy that initializes and pre-fills input sequences with learned image embeddings during inference, preventing error propagation and aligning training with inference.

A dedicated dual-conditioning editing decoder is developed, combining 81-token semantic embeddings from the autoregressive model with 324-token fine-grained embeddings from the input image for robust detail preservation during editing.
paper_summary.summary: Nexus-Gen, developed by Zhejiang University and Alibaba Group, unifies image understanding, generation, and editing within a single 7B-parameter framework, introducing a "prefilled autoregression" strategy to mitigate error accumulation in continuous embedding prediction. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks and releases a new high-quality instruction-guided image editing dataset.

title: Nexus-Gen: Unified Image Understanding, Generation, and Editing via Prefilled Autoregression in Shared Embedding Space

abstract: We introduce OneCAT, a unified multimodal model that seamlessly integrates understanding, generation, and editing within a novel, pure decoder-only transformer architecture. Our framework uniquely eliminates the need for external components such as Vision Transformers (ViT) or vision tokenizer during inference, leading to significant efficiency gains, especially for high-resolution inputs. This is achieved through a modality-specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) structure trained with a single autoregressive (AR) objective, which also natively supports dynamic resolutions. Furthermore, we pioneer a multi-scale visual autoregressive mechanism within the Large Language Model (LLM) that drastically reduces decoding steps compared to diffusion-based methods while maintaining state-of-the-art performance. Our findings demonstrate the powerful potential of pure autoregressive modeling as a sufficient and elegant foundation for unified multimodal intelligence. As a result, OneCAT sets a new performance standard, outperforming existing open-source unified multimodal models across benchmarks for multimodal generation, editing, and understanding.

keyInsights: A novel deep hidden state distillation strategy, utilizing a custom MLLM teacher and matching internal hidden states across all transformer layers, is crucial for transferring comprehensive visual knowledge to encoder-free models.
The Modality-Specific MoE and Scale-Aware Adapter designs within a unified decoder-only framework enable efficient handling of diverse multimodal tasks while maintaining architectural simplicity.
Pure autoregressive modeling with a multi-scale generation mechanism offers a highly efficient alternative to diffusion-based methods for high-quality image generation.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often rely on compositional architectures or external components (vision encoders, VAE tokenizers), leading to high inference latency and information bottlenecks.
Unified visual understanding and generation models using VAE tokenizers suffer from lossy visual information, while hybrid models with diffusion components incur slow generation speeds.
Encoder-free decoder-only MLLMs struggle with effective visual perception and can suffer from supervisory bottlenecks when distilled from smaller teachers.
results: OneCAT-3B achieved state-of-the-art results among unified models, scoring 0.90 on GenEval for fine-grained instruction following and strong performance on DPG-Bench and ImgEdit-Bench for visual generation and editing.
Demonstrated up to 61% faster time-to-first-token for high-resolution image understanding compared to encoder-based MLLMs like Qwen2.5-VL-3B.
Delivered approximately 10x faster visual generation (e.g., 2.85s for text-to-image at 1024x1024) compared to diffusion-based models such as BAGEL-7B.
solution: A pure decoder-only transformer architecture, initialized from Qwen2.5, processes raw visual inputs directly via lightweight patch embeddings, eliminating external vision encoders and VAE tokenizers during inference.
Incorporates a Modality-Specific Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design with specialized FFNs for text, visual understanding, and visual generation, enabling deep and early cross-modal feature fusion.
Integrates a multi-scale autoregressive mechanism (Next-Scale Prediction) with a Scale-Aware Adapter (SAA) directly within the LLM for efficient, coarse-to-fine visual generation.
paper_summary.summary: OneCAT, developed by Meituan Inc. and Shanghai Jiao Tong University, introduces a pure decoder-only, encoder-free, and VAE tokenizer-free multimodal model for unified understanding, generation, and editing. This architecture significantly improves inference efficiency, achieving up to 61% faster time-to-first-token for high-resolution understanding and approximately 10x faster image generation compared to diffusion-based models, while setting new state-of-the-art among unified models on various benchmarks.

title: OneCAT: Decoder-Only Auto-Regressive Model for Unified Understanding and Generation

abstract: We present evaluation results for FLUX.1 Kontext, a generative flow matching model that unifies image generation and editing. The model generates novel output views by incorporating semantic context from text and image inputs. Using a simple sequence concatenation approach, FLUX.1 Kontext handles both local editing and generative in-context tasks within a single unified architecture. Compared to current editing models that exhibit degradation in character consistency and stability across multiple turns, we observe that FLUX.1 Kontext improved preservation of objects and characters, leading to greater robustness in iterative workflows. The model achieves competitive performance with current state-of-the-art systems while delivering significantly faster generation times, enabling interactive applications and rapid prototyping workflows. To validate these improvements, we introduce KontextBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 1026 image-prompt pairs covering five task categories: local editing, global editing, character reference, style reference and text editing. Detailed evaluations show the superior performance of FLUX.1 Kontext in terms of both single-turn quality and multi-turn consistency, setting new standards for unified image processing models.

keyInsights: A simple sequence concatenation approach for handling context images within a transformer backbone effectively unifies text-to-image generation and image-to-image editing tasks.

Rectified flow models, when combined with Latent Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (LADD), can achieve significantly faster inference speeds (3-5 seconds for 1024x1024) while maintaining high output quality.

Joint training on both image-to-image and text-to-image tasks, starting from a pure text-to-image checkpoint, allows for robust performance across both modalities, particularly enhancing character consistency.
originalProblem: Existing instruction-based image editing methods, often trained on synthetic data, tend to produce unrealistic or limited edits and can inherit biases.
Current generative models struggle to maintain consistent character and object identity across multiple, iterative edits, leading to 'visual drift' in multi-turn workflows.
Many powerful autoregressive image generation and editing models suffer from long inference times, hindering interactive applications and rapid prototyping.
results: FLUX.1 Kontext achieves 3-5 second generation times for 1024x1024 images, outperforming competitors by up to an order of magnitude in inference speed.

The model demonstrates superior performance in local editing, text editing, and character consistency on the new KontextBench, evidenced by higher human evaluation scores and AuraFace similarity scores for preserving facial characteristics.

FLUX.1 Kontext exhibits balanced performance across aesthetics, prompt following, typography, and realism in text-to-image generation, improving over its predecessor FLUX1.1 [pro] and reducing the 'AI aesthetic' often seen in outputs.
solution: Built upon FLUX.1, a rectified flow transformer trained in the latent space of a custom convolutional autoencoder for computational efficiency.
Unifies image generation and editing by simply appending context image tokens to target image tokens in the model's visual stream, using positional embedding offsets to differentiate them.
Employs a rectified flow-matching loss for training and incorporates Latent Adversarial Diffusion Distillation (LADD) to significantly speed up sampling and improve sample quality.
paper_summary.summary: Black Forest Labs developed FLUX.1 Kontext, a unified flow-based generative model for both text-to-image generation and image-to-image editing, which achieves 3-5 second generation times for 1024x1024 images and demonstrates superior character consistency during iterative edits. The model was evaluated using KontextBench, a new real-world benchmark spanning diverse editing tasks.

title: FLUX.1 Kontext: Flow Matching for In-Context Image Generation and Editing in Latent Space

abstract: We present a unified transformer, i.e., Show-o, that unifies multimodal understanding and generation. Unlike fully autoregressive models, Show-o unifies autoregressive and (discrete) diffusion modeling to adaptively handle inputs and outputs of various and mixed modalities. The unified model flexibly supports a wide range of vision-language tasks including visual question-answering, text-to-image generation, text-guided inpainting/extrapolation, and mixed-modality generation. Across various benchmarks, it demonstrates comparable or superior performance to existing individual models with an equivalent or larger number of parameters tailored for understanding or generation. This significantly highlights its potential as a next-generation foundation model. Code and models are released at this https URL.

keyInsights: A single transformer can effectively unify multimodal understanding and generation by adaptively applying different optimal modeling paradigms (autoregressive for text, discrete diffusion for images).
This adaptive, unified architecture significantly enhances efficiency in image generation, requiring approximately 20 times fewer sampling steps compared to fully autoregressive models.
By leveraging a pre-trained LLM as its foundation, the model can inherently encode text conditional information and maintain strong language capabilities while learning robust cross-modal alignment.
originalProblem: Existing unified multimodal models typically rely on fragmented architectures with multiple specialized 'expert' components, leading to increased complexity and redundancy.

Prior single-transformer approaches for multimodal unification, like Chameleon, suffered from inefficient image generation due to fully autoregressive modeling requiring a prohibitively large number of sampling steps.

There was a need for a truly unified foundational model that could intelligently adapt its modeling approach (e.g., sequential for text, parallel denoising for images) to different modalities within a single coherent architecture.
results: Achieved comparable multimodal understanding performance to dedicated understanding-only models (e.g., LLaVA-v1.5-Phi-1.5) and outperformed or matched larger unified models across various benchmarks (POPE, MME, VQAv2, GQA) with only 1.3B parameters.
Demonstrated superior text-to-image generation quality, achieving a FID-30K of 9.24 on MSCOCO and an overall GenEval score of 0.68, outperforming several larger generation-only and unified models.

Natively supports novel mixed-modality applications like text-guided image inpainting, extrapolation, and interleaved video keyframe generation without specific fine-tuning, showcasing its versatility.
solution: Show-o is built upon a single pre-trained Large Language Model (LLM) backbone, expanded with unified discrete tokenization for both text and images, allowing for a shared vocabulary.

An innovative 'Omni-Attention' mechanism dynamically switches between causal attention for text tokens and full attention for image tokens, enabling adaptive processing within the unified transformer.

The model integrates two distinct training objectives: Next Token Prediction (NTP) for autoregressive text tasks and Mask Token Prediction (MTP) for discrete diffusion-based image generation, combining them into a single training pipeline.
paper_summary.summary: Show-o, developed by Show Lab at NUS and ByteDance, presents a single transformer capable of unifying multimodal understanding and generation by adaptively combining autoregressive modeling for text and discrete diffusion for images. The model achieves competitive performance across various vision-language benchmarks with only 1.3 billion parameters and significantly improves image generation efficiency with fewer sampling steps.

title: Show-o: One Single Transformer to Unify Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract: Unified multimodal models have shown promising results in multimodal content generation and editing but remain largely limited to the image domain. In this work, we present UniVideo, a versatile framework that extends unified modeling to the video domain. UniVideo adopts a dual-stream design, combining a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for instruction understanding with a Multimodal DiT (MMDiT) for video generation. This design preserves the MLLM's original text generation capabilities, enables accurate interpretation of complex multimodal instructions, and maintains visual consistency in the generated content. Built on this architecture, UniVideo unifies diverse video generation and editing tasks under a single multimodal instruction paradigm and is jointly trained across them. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniVideo matches or surpasses state-of-the-art task-specific baselines in text/image-to-video generation, in-context video generation and in-context video editing. Notably, the unified design of UniVideo enables two forms of generalization. First, UniVideo supports task composition, such as combining editing with style transfer, by integrating multiple capabilities within a single instruction. Second, even without explicit training on free-form video editing, UniVideo transfers its editing capability from large-scale image editing data to this setting, handling unseen instructions such as changing the environment or altering materials within a video. Beyond these core capabilities, UniVideo also supports visual-prompt-based video generation, where the MLLM interprets visual prompts and guides the MMDiT during synthesis. To foster future research, we released our model and code.

keyInsights: A dual-stream architecture, integrating a frozen MLLM for high-level semantic understanding and a fine-tuned MM-DiT for visual generation, effectively addresses the diverse requirements of video tasks.
Unifying various video generation and editing tasks through a multimodal instruction paradigm fosters emergent capabilities, such as zero-shot generalization to unseen free-form editing scenarios.

Direct visual input to the generative branch via a VAE, in addition to MLLM-derived semantic conditioning, is critical for maintaining high-fidelity visual details and consistent identity during video manipulation.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI primarily focused on text and images, leaving video tasks fragmented and underexplored within a unified framework.
Current video generation models are largely task-specific (e.g., text-to-video) and struggle with complex, multimodal instructions that involve images or other visual cues.
Video editing typically relies on dedicated, task-specific modules or pipelines, limiting scalability and adaptability to diverse, mask-free editing scenarios.
results: UniVideo achieves a VBench score of 82.58 for T2V/I2V generation, closely matching the performance of a specialized 13B backbone model.

It outperforms or competes with state-of-the-art open-source and commercial models in human evaluation metrics for in-context video generation and mask-free video editing, often without requiring explicit masks.

The model shows zero-shot generalization to novel tasks, including free-form video editing (e.g., material or environment changes) and compositional tasks, and effectively interprets complex visual prompts.
solution: Develops UniVideo, a unified framework that integrates video understanding, generation, and editing capabilities into a single model.

Implements a dual-stream architecture featuring a frozen qwen2.5VL-7B Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for high-level semantic understanding and a fine-tuned HunyuanVideo-T2V-13B Multimodal DiT (MM-DiT) for visual generation.
Leverages a three-stage training strategy to align the MLLM's outputs with the MM-DiT and enable a broad spectrum of video tasks through multimodal instruction following.
paper_summary.summary: UniVideo introduces a unified multimodal generative model capable of video understanding, generation, and editing from diverse instructions. The model demonstrates competitive performance across various video tasks and exhibits zero-shot generalization for free-form video editing, surpassing specialized baselines in several human evaluation metrics.

title: UniVideo: Unified Understanding, Generation, and Editing for Videos

abstract: The prevailing Next-Token Prediction (NTP) paradigm has driven the success of large language models through discrete autoregressive modeling. However, contemporary multimodal systems remain language-centric, often treating non-linguistic modalities as external attachments, leading to fragmented architectures and suboptimal integration. To transcend this limitation, we introduce Discrete Native Autoregressive (DiNA), a unified framework that represents multimodal information within a shared discrete space, enabling a consistent and principled autoregressive modeling across modalities. A key innovation is the Discrete Native Any-resolution Visual Transformer (dNaViT), which performs tokenization and de-tokenization at arbitrary resolutions, transforming continuous visual signals into hierarchical discrete tokens. Building on this foundation, we develop LongCat-Next, a native multimodal model that processes text, vision, and audio under a single autoregressive objective with minimal modality-specific design. As an industrial-strength foundation model, it excels at seeing, painting, and talking within a single framework, achieving strong performance across a wide range of multimodal benchmarks. In particular, LongCat-Next addresses the long-standing performance ceiling of discrete vision modeling on understanding tasks and provides a unified approach to effectively reconcile the conflict between understanding and generation. As an attempt toward native multimodality, we open-source the LongCat-Next and its tokenizers, hoping to foster further research and development in the community. GitHub: this https URL

keyInsights: Treating multimodality as a native linguistic extension allows all modalities to be represented as interoperable token sequences under a single autoregressive objective, streamlining model architecture.

Achieving 'semantic completeness' in discrete visual tokens through SAEs and RVQ is crucial for retaining sufficient information for both discriminative understanding and high-fidelity generative tasks.

A unified model trained with a balanced mixture of understanding and generation data can lead to synergistic performance, where generation capabilities are enhanced without compromising understanding.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal systems often treat non-linguistic modalities (vision, audio) as external components, leading to fragmented architectures and suboptimal integration with language models.
Representing continuous, high-dimensional non-linguistic modalities within a shared discrete token space poses a challenge in preserving information fidelity for both understanding and generation.
Current visual tokenization methods struggle to simultaneously achieve high-level conceptual reasoning, explicit semantic grounding for generation, and avoid redundancy or information loss.
results: LongCat-Next achieved top performance on visual understanding benchmarks, scoring 83.1 on MathVista, 64.7 on MathVision, and 70.6 on MMMU, surpassing many specialist MLLMs.

The model demonstrated competitive text-to-image generation performance, outperforming prior unified multimodal models across most benchmarks and competing favorably with specialized systems, particularly in text rendering.

It showcased strong audio capabilities, including a 1.63 Word Error Rate on LibriSpeech test-clean for ASR, 1.90 WER on SeedTTS_zh for TTS, and robust performance in various audio comprehension tasks.
solution: Introduced the Discrete Native Autoregressive (DiNA) framework, extending the next-token prediction paradigm to unify multimodal information representation within a shared discrete space.

Developed dNaViT, a unified visual tokenizer, which uses Semantic-and-Aligned Encoders (SAE) and Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to create hierarchical discrete 'visual words' from any-resolution inputs.

Designed an audio tokenizer to convert continuous speech into discrete tokens using a Whisper encoder and an 8-layer RVQ, preserving both semantic and acoustic information for LLM integration and reconstruction.
paper_summary.summary: The Meituan LongCat Team presents LongCat-Next, a unified multimodal model leveraging a Discrete Native Autoregressive (DiNA) framework to convert diverse inputs like vision and audio into discrete tokens. This single model demonstrates competitive capabilities across visual understanding (e.g., 83.1 on MathVista), image generation, and audio tasks (e.g., 1.63 WER on LibriSpeech test-clean).

title: LongCat-Next: Lexicalizing Modalities as Discrete Tokens

abstract: Bridging different modalities lies at the heart of cross-modality generation. While conventional approaches treat the text modality as a conditioning signal that gradually guides the denoising process from Gaussian noise to the target image modality, we explore a much simpler paradigm-directly evolving between text and image modalities through flow matching. This requires projecting both modalities into a shared latent space, which poses a significant challenge due to their inherently different representations: text is highly semantic and encoded as 1D tokens, whereas images are spatially redundant and represented as 2D latent embeddings. To address this, we introduce FlowTok, a minimal framework that seamlessly flows across text and images by encoding images into a compact 1D token representation. Compared to prior methods, this design reduces the latent space size by 3.3x at an image resolution of 256, eliminating the need for complex conditioning mechanisms or noise scheduling. Moreover, FlowTok naturally extends to image-to-text generation under the same formulation. With its streamlined architecture centered around compact 1D tokens, FlowTok is highly memory-efficient, requires significantly fewer training resources, and achieves much faster sampling speeds-all while delivering performance comparable to state-of-the-art models. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Representing both text and images as compact, unified 1D latent tokens enables a simpler and more efficient paradigm for cross-modal generation.
Direct flow matching between these aligned 1D token sequences streamlines the generative process, significantly reducing computational overhead for both training and inference.
Learning an effective text projection into the compact latent space, aided by a CLIP-style contrastive alignment loss, is crucial for maintaining semantic fidelity during cross-modal transformations.
originalProblem: Prevailing text-to-image (T2I) models, primarily diffusion-based, rely on complex conditioning mechanisms and intricate noise scheduling, leading to high computational costs.
Existing flow matching models for cross-modal generation often utilize 2D latent representations for images, which can still incur substantial computational expenses and slower inference.
A conceptual gap exists between multimodal understanding, which projects modalities into shared latent spaces, and multimodal generation, which traditionally employs conditional approaches.
results: FlowTok-H attained a COCO FID-30K of 9.67 and MJHQ-30K FID of 7.15, demonstrating competitive image generation quality compared to leading models, even when trained on public datasets.

Training costs were substantially reduced, with FlowTok-H requiring 26.1 8-A100 days, and inference speed increased by up to 20x compared to prior flow matching approaches, reaching 18.2 images/second for FlowTok-H.

The framework achieved superior memory efficiency, supporting large batch sizes (8K on 8 A100 GPUs) without requiring gradient checkpointing, and extended successfully to image-to-text generation, achieving a BLEU-4 of 37.1 and CIDEr of 117.0 on the COCO Karpathy split.
solution: Images are encoded into compact 1D tokens using an enhanced visual tokenizer, aligning the number of tokens with text encoder output.
Text embeddings are projected into a matching low-dimensional 1D latent space via a lightweight Transformer network, regularized by KL divergence and an auxiliary text alignment loss.
A direct flow matching approach with stacked DiT blocks is applied, where the source modality's 1D tokens directly serve as the source distribution, eliminating complex conditioning.
paper_summary.summary: The FlowTok framework streamlines cross-modal generative AI by projecting both text and image modalities into a unified, compact 1D latent space for direct flow matching. This approach enables competitive text-to-image generation quality while drastically reducing training expenses to 20.4 A100-days and accelerating inference speed up to 20 times compared to prior methods.

title: FlowTok: Flowing Seamlessly Across Text and Image Tokens

abstract: We present VINO, a unified visual generator that performs image and video generation and editing within a single framework. Instead of relying on task-specific models or independent modules for each modality, VINO uses a shared diffusion backbone that conditions on text, images and videos, enabling a broad range of visual creation and editing tasks under one model. Specifically, VINO couples a vision-language model (VLM) with a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT), where multimodal inputs are encoded as interleaved conditioning tokens, and then used to guide the diffusion process. This design supports multi-reference grounding, long-form instruction following, and coherent identity preservation across static and dynamic content, while avoiding modality-specific architectural components. To train such a unified system, we introduce a multi-stage training pipeline that progressively expands a video generation base model into a unified, multi-task generator capable of both image and video input and output. Across diverse generation and editing benchmarks, VINO demonstrates strong visual quality, faithful instruction following, improved reference and attribute preservation, and more controllable multi-identity edits. Our results highlight a practical path toward scalable unified visual generation, and the promise of interleaved, in-context computation as a foundation for general-purpose visual creation.

keyInsights: Coupling a powerful, frozen VLM with a diffusion backbone can effectively unify and interpret diverse multimodal conditioning signals, allowing a single model to address various visual generation and editing tasks.

Explicitly marking boundaries between different visual latent inputs (images vs. videos) using a token-boundary mechanism within the diffusion model is crucial for correctly disentangling and grounding multiple visual references without artifacts.

A progressive multi-stage training strategy is essential for developing a robust unified multi-task generative model, enabling efficient skill acquisition (e.g., editing) without degrading performance on foundational tasks (e.g., text-to-video generation).
originalProblem: Visual generation pipelines are fragmented, requiring separate models for tasks like text-to-image, text-to-video, and various visual editing, which leads to inefficiencies.
Existing multimodal models struggle to reliably process multiple, simultaneously provided conditioning signals (text, images, videos) without semantic conflicts or inconsistencies.

A unified model needs to robustly handle diverse task requirements, such as long descriptive captions for generation versus short imperative instructions for editing, and effectively perform multi-reference grounding.
results: VInO successfully achieved unified image and video generation and editing within a single framework, preserving strong text-to-image and text-to-video generation capabilities comparable to its specialized backbone.

The model demonstrated superior performance on reference-based video generation, surpassing several closed-source systems on the OpenS2V benchmark, and significantly outperformed all competing open-source methods on instruction-based image and video editing benchmarks (ImgEdit, GEdit, OpenVE-Bench).

Ablation studies confirmed that learnable tokens are crucial for stable optimization and semantic precision, and the token-boundary mechanism is essential for correct disentanglement of multimodal VAE latents, preventing structural distortions and ensuring robust grounding.
solution: VInO integrates a frozen Vision-Language Model (Qwen3VL-4B-Instruction) as a universal front-end interpreter with a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer (MMDiT, initialized with HunyuanVideo) as the core generative engine.

An 'interleaved omnimodal context' is implemented using learnable query tokens, direct VAE latent injection, and a token-boundary mechanism within the MMDiT to robustly disentangle and integrate diverse visual and textual control signals.

A progressive multi-stage training pipeline is employed, starting with VLM-MMDiT alignment, then short prompt adaptation, and finally full multi-task mixed training to acquire diverse capabilities and prevent catastrophic forgetting.
paper_summary.summary: VInO is a unified visual generator developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Nanyang Technology University, and Kuaishou Technology's Kling Team, capable of both image and video creation and editing tasks through the integration of a Vision-Language Model with a Multimodal Diffusion Transformer. The model exhibits strong performance across various benchmarks, outperforming several open-source methods and even some closed-source systems on reference-based video generation.

title: VINO: A Unified Visual Generator with Interleaved OmniModal Context

abstract:
Flow matching models have emerged as a strong alternative to diffusion
models, but existing inversion and editing methods designed for diffusion are
often ineffective or inapplicable to them. The straight-line, non-crossing
trajectories of flow models pose challenges for diffusion-based approaches but
also open avenues for novel solutions. In this paper, we introduce a
predictor-corrector-based framework for inversion and editing in flow models.
First, we propose Uni-Inv, an effective inversion method designed for accurate
reconstruction. Building on this, we extend the concept of delayed injection to
flow models and introduce Uni-Edit, a region-aware, robust image editing
approach. Our methodology is tuning-free, model-agnostic, efficient, and
effective, enabling diverse edits while ensuring strong preservation of
edit-irrelevant regions. Extensive experiments across various generative models
demonstrate the superiority and generalizability of Uni-Inv and Uni-Edit, even
under low-cost settings. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Flow models' straight-line, non-crossing trajectories can be leveraged for more effective editing
Region-adaptive guidance masks help preserve edit-irrelevant areas while enabling targeted modifications
Model-agnostic design allows techniques to work across different architectures
originalProblem: Existing image inversion and editing techniques designed for diffusion models perform poorly on flow models
Flow models have unique characteristics (straight-line trajectories) that require specialized approaches for effective manipulation
results: Achieves exact reconstruction of real images while outperforming existing inversion methods
Demonstrates effective editing across multiple models (Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX) without requiring model-specific tuning
Maintains computational efficiency through tuning-free approach that avoids iterative optimization
solution: Uni-Inv: A predictor-corrector framework for accurate image reconstruction that minimizes discretization errors
Uni-Edit: Region-aware editing approach using delayed injection and velocity fusion to control modifications
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University, UBC, and Snap Inc. introduce UniEdit-Flow, a framework enabling accurate image inversion and editing for flow-based generative models through a predictor-corrector approach and region-adaptive guidance, achieving exact reconstruction while maintaining edit-irrelevant regions across multiple model architectures without requiring additional training.

title: UniEdit-Flow: Unleashing Inversion and Editing in the Era of Flow Models

abstract: Unified models capable of interleaved generation have emerged as a promising paradigm, with the community increasingly converging on autoregressive modeling for text and flow matching for image generation. To advance this direction, we propose a unified reinforcement learning framework tailored for interleaved generation. We validate our approach on its fundamental unit: a single round of reasoning-driven image generation, where the model first expands the user prompt through reasoning, followed by image synthesis. Formulating this multimodal generation process as a Markov Decision Process with sparse terminal rewards, we introduce UniGRPO to jointly optimize text and image generation policies using GRPO. Adopting a minimalist methodology to avoid over-design, we leverage established training recipes for both modalities by seamlessly integrating standard GRPO for reasoning and FlowGRPO for visual synthesis. To ensure scalability to multi-round interleaved generation, we introduce two critical modifications to the original FlowGRPO: (1) eliminating classifier-free guidance to maintain linear, unbranched rollouts, which is essential for scaling to complex scenarios involving multi-turn interactions and multi-condition generation (e.g., editing); and (2) replacing the standard latent KL penalty with an MSE penalty directly on the velocity fields, providing a more robust and direct regularization signal to mitigate reward hacking effectively. Our experiments demonstrate that this unified training recipe significantly enhances image generation quality through reasoning, providing a robust and scalable baseline for the future post-training of fully interleaved models.

keyInsights: Jointly optimizing both reasoning (text) and visual synthesis policies within a unified RL framework yields greater performance gains compared to optimizing either component in isolation.

Training without Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) can simplify the computational pipeline and support scalability for multi-round scenarios without compromising final performance when CFG is applied at evaluation time.
Velocity-based MSE regularization on unweighted velocity fields provides superior robustness against reward hacking and ensures stable training dynamics for flow-matching models in an RL setting.
originalProblem: Developing a unified RL framework that can jointly optimize both discrete language actions and continuous visual actions within a single, coherent optimization loop for multi-round interleaved generation.
Addressing the computational and gradient complexity introduced by Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) and challenges of reward hacking in visual RL within a unified multimodal context.
Creating a scalable and robust approach for post-training fully interleaved models with general-purpose prompts, rather than employing separate models or disjoint optimization loops.
results: UniGRPO achieved the highest performance across all evaluated methods, scoring 0.8381 on the Text Alignment benchmark and 0.90 on GenEval, outperforming baselines and isolated optimization approaches.

Qualitative analysis demonstrated UniGRPO's ability to generate photorealistic and finely detailed images that accurately reflect complex prompts, and to optimize reasoning traces to be purposeful and task-oriented.

Ablation studies confirmed that the proposed velocity-based MSE regularization ensured stable training and high-fidelity images, while CFG-free training provided comparable or superior final performance against standard CFG-enabled training.
solution:
UniGRPO formulates the entire "Prompt 
Thinking 
Image" sequence as a single Markov Decision Process (MDP) to jointly optimize text and image generation.
It integrates standard GRPO for autoregressive text and FlowGRPO (which adapts RL to flow matching via SDEs) for visual synthesis into a unified objective function.

Critical modifications include eliminating Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) during training for linear rollouts and replacing standard latent KL regularization with a velocity-based Mean Squared Error (MSE) penalty to prevent reward hacking.
paper_summary.summary: A unified Reinforcement Learning framework, UniGRPO, jointly optimizes discrete language and continuous visual policies for interleaved multimodal generation, achieving superior performance on text alignment and compositional benchmarks. The framework integrates GRPO for text and FlowGRPO for images, while introducing key modifications for scalability and stability.

title: UniGRPO: Unified Policy Optimization for Reasoning-Driven Visual Generation

abstract: Recent advances in foundation models highlight a clear trend toward unification and scaling, showing emergent capabilities across diverse domains. While image generation and editing have rapidly transitioned from task-specific to unified frameworks, video generation and editing remain fragmented due to architectural limitations and data scarcity. In this work, we introduce EditVerse, a unified framework for image and video generation and editing within a single model. By representing all modalities, i.e., text, image, and video, as a unified token sequence, EditVerse leverages self-attention to achieve robust in-context learning, natural cross-modal knowledge transfer, and flexible handling of inputs and outputs with arbitrary resolutions and durations. To address the lack of video editing training data, we design a scalable data pipeline that curates 232K video editing samples and combines them with large-scale image and video datasets for joint training. Furthermore, we present EditVerseBench, the first benchmark for instruction-based video editing covering diverse tasks and resolutions. Extensive experiments and user studies demonstrate that EditVerse achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing existing open-source and commercial models, while exhibiting emergent editing and generation abilities across modalities.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: A unified transformer architecture with full self-attention over interleaved text and vision sequences can effectively enable in-context learning and cross-modal knowledge transfer between image and video domains.
Sophisticated data generation, curation, and filtering pipelines are crucial for overcoming data scarcity and ensuring quality in complex multimodal tasks like instruction-based video editing.
Appropriate positional embeddings, such as the developed 4D RoPE, are essential for effectively encoding diverse spatial, sequential, and temporal information within a unified transformer model.
originalProblem: Existing image and video editing and generation models are fragmented and task-specific, lacking a unified approach.
Video generation and editing capabilities are hindered by architectural limitations and a scarcity of high-quality, diverse, instruction-based video editing datasets.
The absence of a comprehensive benchmark for instruction-based video editing made it difficult to evaluate generalist models effectively.
results: EditVerse achieves state-of-the-art performance on the newly introduced EditVerseBench, outperforming existing research and commercial models in editing quality, video quality, text alignment, and temporal consistency.

The model demonstrates emergent capabilities, performing out-of-distribution editing tasks (e.g., "change material," "add effects") and generating results that surpass the quality of its own training data.

Ablation studies confirm that cross-modal transfer from image generation and editing data significantly improves video editing performance, and that the interleaved input formulation with sequential RoPE is critical for text alignment and overall editing quality.
solution: EditVerse employs a 2B-parameter dense transformer with full self-attention, designed to process interleaved text and vision tokens for a wide array of image and video tasks.
A four-dimensional Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) is introduced to effectively encode spatial, sequential, and temporal relationships across interleaved modalities.
A scalable data pipeline automatically generates high-quality video editing data, leveraging cross-modal knowledge transfer from abundant image data to mitigate video data scarcity.
paper_summary.summary: EditVerse introduces a unified 2B-parameter transformer framework, developed by Adobe Research in collaboration with The Chinese University of Hong Kong and Johns Hopkins University, that performs image and video editing and generation using natural language instructions. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on a new instruction-based video editing benchmark and demonstrates emergent capabilities by leveraging a full self-attention mechanism over an interleaved token sequence and a novel data generation pipeline.

title: EditVerse: Unifying Image and Video Editing and Generation with In-Context Learning

abstract: Unified video modeling that combines generation and understanding capabilities is increasingly important but faces two key challenges: maintaining semantic faithfulness during flow-based generation due to text-visual token imbalance and the limitations of uniform cross-modal attention across the flow trajectory, and efficiently extending image-centric MLLMs to video without costly retraining. We present UniVid, a unified architecture that couples an MLLM with a diffusion decoder through a lightweight adapter, enabling both video understanding and generation. We introduce Temperature Modality Alignment to improve prompt adherence and Pyramid Reflection for efficient temporal reasoning via dynamic keyframe selection. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving a 2.2% improvement on VBench-Long total score compared to EasyAnimateV5.1, and 1.0% and 3.3% accuracy gains on MSVD-QA and ActivityNet-QA, respectively, compared with the best prior 7B baselines. Code: this https URL. Website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Dynamically modulating cross-modal attention based on denoising timestep, especially strengthening textual signals early, effectively mitigates text suppression and improves semantic fidelity in video generation.

Video understanding can be significantly enhanced by framing keyframe selection as a test-time reinforcement learning problem, allowing adaptive evidence retrieval and verbal policy improvement for refined temporal reasoning.

A hybrid diffusion-AR paradigm can efficiently leverage powerful pre-trained components (MLLMs and diffusion models) with minimal architectural modifications to achieve competitive performance across both video generation and understanding.
originalProblem: Existing flow-based video generation models struggle to maintain strong semantic adherence to text prompts, particularly over longer or higher-resolution videos, leading to prompt-video drift.
Adapting image-centric MLLMs for video understanding is computationally expensive and inefficient, often processing all frames uniformly or relying on fixed sampling that can miss critical evidence.
results: UniVid achieved a state-of-the-art overall VBench-Long score of 85.27, demonstrating superior semantic alignment (80.58) and high temporal consistency (99.88) in video generation.
It established new state-of-the-art for 7B-scale models on MSVD-QA (80.1% accuracy) and ActivityNet-QA (58.8% accuracy), while remaining competitive on MSRVTT-QA and TGIF-QA.

Ablation studies confirmed that Temperature Modality Alignment is crucial for semantic fidelity and temporal coherence in generation, and the Pyramid Reflection loop significantly boosts accuracy in understanding through improved evidence retrieval.
solution: Introduced Temperature Modality Alignment (TMA), a timestep-aware, temperature-adjusted cross-modal attention schedule that amplifies textual guidance during early denoising phases in generation.

Developed Pyramid Reflection, a novel query-driven, hierarchical test-time reinforcement learning loop that iteratively selects and refines keyframe context for efficient video understanding without modifying MLLM backbones.

Proposed a unified architecture that strategically couples an MLLM (BAGEL-7B) with a diffusion video decoder (Wan 2.2) via a lightweight conditioning adapter, trained through a three-stage hierarchical recipe.
paper_summary.summary: UniVid, an open-source unified video model, integrates multimodal LLMs with a diffusion video decoder to excel in both video generation and understanding tasks. It achieves a state-of-the-art overall VBench-Long score of 85.27 and sets new SOTA for 7B-scale models on MSVD-QA (80.1% accuracy) and ActivityNet-QA (58.8% accuracy), enhancing semantic faithfulness in generation and efficient temporal reasoning in understanding.

title: UniVid: The Open-Source Unified Video Model

abstract: We present OneFlow, the first non-autoregressive multimodal model that enables variable-length and concurrent mixed-modal generation. Unlike autoregressive models that enforce rigid causal ordering between text and image generation, OneFlow combines an insertion-based Edit Flow for discrete text tokens with Flow Matching for image latents. OneFlow enables concurrent text-image synthesis with hierarchical sampling that prioritizes content over grammar. Through controlled experiments across model sizes from 1B to 8B, we demonstrate that OneFlow outperforms autoregressive baselines on both generation and understanding tasks while using up to 50% fewer training FLOPs. OneFlow surpasses both autoregressive and diffusion-based approaches while unlocking new capabilities for concurrent generation, iterative refinement, and natural reasoning-like generation.

keyInsights: Non-autoregressive architectures can exhibit emergent reasoning capabilities, naturally generating intermediate steps when prompted, without explicit Chain-of-Thought or RL finetuning.
Concurrent mixed-modal pretraining significantly boosts downstream performance, showing relative improvements of 4% on VQA and 1.5% on image generation over sequential pretraining.

Unifying discrete and continuous generative paradigms within a single non-autoregressive framework offers substantial efficiency gains and superior scaling compared to traditional autoregressive methods.
originalProblem: Autoregressive multimodal models impose a rigid causal ordering, preventing simultaneous cross-modal refinement and leading to inefficiencies.
Diffusion-based multimodal models are restricted to predetermined single text-image pairs and often fixed modality assignments, lacking support for variable-length interleaved sequences.
Existing models do not effectively support the simultaneous generation of variable-length interleaved sequences of text and images.
results: OneFlow achieves competitive performance with up to 50% fewer training FLOPs compared to autoregressive + Flow Matching baselines across various benchmarks.
The 1B parameter model nearly matches the VQA performance of an 8B MMaDA model, and the 8B OneFlow model surpasses MMaDA 8B on most benchmarks without extensive post-training.

It unlocks novel capabilities such as applying Classifier-Free Guidance to text insertion rates for controlling detail and enabling truly simultaneous interleaved generation of text and images, allowing them to co-evolve.
solution: OneFlow integrates insertion-based Edit Flows for discrete text generation and Flow Matching for continuous image generation, powered by a shared Transformer architecture.
It introduces a novel interleaved time schedule, allowing for dynamic insertion and concurrent refinement of both text and image elements throughout the generation process.
The framework operates non-autoregressively, overcoming the sequential constraints of previous models to enable flexible, mixed-modal, and variable-length output generation.
paper_summary.summary: The OneFlow framework enables concurrent mixed-modal and interleaved text and image generation by unifying discrete Edit Flows for text and continuous Flow Matching for images within a shared Transformer backbone. It scales more efficiently than autoregressive baselines, achieving similar performance with up to 50% fewer training FLOPs.

title: OneFlow: Concurrent Mixed-Modal and Interleaved Generation with Edit Flows

abstract:
Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion models have shown impressive results in
generating visually compelling images following user prompts. Building on this,
various methods further fine-tune the pre-trained T2I model for specific tasks.
However, this requires separate model architectures, training designs, and
multiple parameter sets to handle different tasks. In this paper, we introduce
UniVG, a generalist diffusion model capable of supporting a diverse range of
image generation tasks with a single set of weights. UniVG treats multi-modal
inputs as unified conditions to enable various downstream applications, ranging
from T2I generation, inpainting, instruction-based editing, identity-preserving
generation, and layout-guided generation, to depth estimation and referring
segmentation. Through comprehensive empirical studies on data mixing and
multi-task training, we provide detailed insights into the training processes
and decisions that inform our final designs. For example, we show that T2I
generation and other tasks, such as instruction-based editing, can coexist
without performance trade-offs, while auxiliary tasks like depth estimation and
referring segmentation enhance image editing. Notably, our model can even
outperform some task-specific models on their respective benchmarks, marking a
significant step towards a unified image generation model.
title: UniVG: A Generalist Diffusion Model for Unified Image Generation and  Editing

abstract:
We present JanusFlow, a powerful framework that unifies image understanding
and generation in a single model. JanusFlow introduces a minimalist
architecture that integrates autoregressive language models with rectified
flow, a state-of-the-art method in generative modeling. Our key finding
demonstrates that rectified flow can be straightforwardly trained within the
large language model framework, eliminating the need for complex architectural
modifications. To further improve the performance of our unified model, we
adopt two key strategies: (i) decoupling the understanding and generation
encoders, and (ii) aligning their representations during unified training.
Extensive experiments show that JanusFlow achieves comparable or superior
performance to specialized models in their respective domains, while
significantly outperforming existing unified approaches across standard
benchmarks. This work represents a step toward more efficient and versatile
vision-language models.

keyInsights: Decoupling visual encoders for understanding (using a pre-trained semantic encoder) and generation (using a scratch-initialized encoder) effectively mitigates task interference, leading to improved performance in both domains.

A novel representation alignment regularization technique significantly enhances the semantic consistency and quality of generated images by aligning the LLM's intermediate representations during generation with the understanding encoder's semantic features.

Integrating rectified flow directly within an autoregressive LLM offers a minimalist yet powerful approach for unified multimodal understanding and generation, avoiding the complexities of external generative models or discrete image tokenization.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI models often separate image understanding (MLLMs) and image generation (diffusion models), leading to fragmented architectures.
Unified multimodal models frequently suffer from architectural complexity (e.g., relying on external pre-trained generative models) or limited image quality (e.g., due to discrete tokenization).
A single model attempting both understanding and generation often faces 'task interference,' where optimizing for one task compromises the other, particularly when sharing visual encoders.
results: JanusFlow achieved an overall GenEval score of 0.63, outperforming most previous unified models and several generation-specific models like SDv1.5 and DALL-E 2.
The model scored 80.09% on DPG-Bench, competitive with or surpassing established generation-specific models such as SDXL and Playground v2.5 for fine-grained semantic accuracy.

JanusFlow achieved state-of-the-art understanding performance among unified models with similar parameter counts, often exceeding larger understanding-specific models (e.g., outperforming LLaVA-v1.5 7B on MMBench and SEEDBench).
solution: JanusFlow introduces a unified framework based on an autoregressive DeepSeek-LLM (1.3B parameters) that directly integrates rectified flow for image generation.
It employs decoupled visual encoders: a pre-trained SigLIP for understanding tasks ($f_{enc}$) and a separate, scratch-initialized ConvNeXt-based encoder for generation tasks ($g_{enc}$).

A representation alignment regularization ($L_{REPA}$) is introduced to align intermediate LLM representations during generation with the semantic features from the understanding encoder, improving coherence and quality.
paper_summary.summary: JanusFlow, developed by DeepSeek-AI and collaborators, presents a unified framework that combines an autoregressive Large Language Model with rectified flow for both multimodal understanding and image generation. The model achieves competitive performance against specialized models and sets new state-of-the-art benchmarks among unified approaches by using decoupled visual encoders and a novel representation alignment regularization.

title: JanusFlow: Harmonizing Autoregression and Rectified Flow for Unified  Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract: Despite significant progress in diffusion-based image generation, subject-driven generation and instruction-based editing remain challenging. Existing methods typically treat them separately, struggling with limited high-quality data and poor generalization. However, both tasks require capturing complex visual variations while maintaining consistency between inputs and outputs. Inspired by this, we propose MIGE, a unified framework that standardizes task representations using multimodal instructions. It first treats subject-driven generation as creation on a blank canvas and instruction-based editing as modification of an existing image, establishing a shared input-output formulation, then introduces a novel multimodal encoder that maps free-form multimodal instructions into a unified vision-language space, integrating visual and semantic features through a feature fusion mechanism. This unification enables joint training of both tasks, providing two key advantages: (1) Cross-Task Enhancement: by leveraging shared visual and semantic representations, joint training improves instruction adherence and visual consistency in both subject-driven generation and instruction-based editing. (2) Generalization: learning in a unified format facilitates cross-task knowledge transfer, enabling MIGE to generalize to novel compositional tasks, including instruction-based subject-driven editing. Experiments show that MIGE excels in both subject-driven generation and instruction-based editing while setting a SOTA in the new task of instruction-based subject-driven editing. Code and model have been publicly available at this https URL.
title: MIGE: Mutually Enhanced Multimodal Instruction-Based Image Generation and Editing

abstract: The rapid progress of large language models (LLMs) has catalyzed the emergence of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that unify visual understanding and image generation within a single framework. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive (AR) architectures, which impose inherent limitations on future development, such as the raster-scan order in image generation and restricted reasoning abilities in causal context modeling. In this work, we challenge the dominance of AR-based approaches by introducing FUDOKI, a unified multimodal model purely based on discrete flow matching, as an alternative to conventional AR paradigms. By leveraging metric-induced probability paths with kinetic optimal velocities, our framework goes beyond the previous masking-based corruption process, enabling iterative refinement with self-correction capability and richer bidirectional context integration during generation. To mitigate the high cost of training from scratch, we initialize FUDOKI from pre-trained AR-based MLLMs and adaptively transition to the discrete flow matching paradigm. Experimental results show that FUDOKI achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art AR-based MLLMs across both visual understanding and image generation tasks, highlighting its potential as a foundation for next-generation unified multimodal models. Furthermore, we show that applying test-time scaling techniques to FUDOKI yields significant performance gains, further underscoring its promise for future enhancement through reinforcement learning.

keyInsights: Discrete Flow Matching with kinetic optimal velocities enables iterative refinement and continuous self-correction in MLLMs, addressing limitations of autoregressive and masking-based models.
Pre-training from autoregressive MLLMs can efficiently initialize and accelerate the training of discrete flow-based models, making large-scale development more feasible.
Explicit time embedding layers are not always necessary, as the model can implicitly infer timesteps from metric-induced corrupted inputs.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), primarily based on autoregressive (AR) architectures, suffer from rigid raster-scan generation order, unidirectional context, and difficulties in self-correction or complex reasoning.
Discrete generative models, while promising, often rely on masking-based corruption processes that prevent modification of tokens once unmasked, hindering iterative refinement.
Training large, novel MLLM architectures from scratch is computationally expensive and resource-intensive.
results: FUDOKI achieved a competitive 0.77 overall score on the GenEval benchmark for visual generation and strong performance on DPG-Bench, demonstrating superior text-image alignment and visual fidelity compared to AR and mask-based baselines.
The model scored 1485.4 on MME-P and 73.9 on MMB, showing robust multimodal understanding capabilities competitive with or surpassing AR-based MLLMs of similar or larger scale.
FUDOKI qualitatively demonstrated self-correction in reasoning processes and improved multi-step task completion, with test-time inference scaling further boosting GenEval to 0.88 and MM-Vet to 55.5.
solution: Introduces FUDOKI, a unified multimodal model built purely on discrete flow matching (DFM) that learns a continuous transformation from a source to target distribution in discrete space.

Leverages metric-induced probability paths and kinetic optimal velocities to define a semantically meaningful corruption process that enables iterative refinement and bidirectional context integration.
Employs a two-stage adaptive transfer strategy, initializing from pre-trained autoregressive MLLMs (Janus-1.5B) to efficiently adapt the model to the DFM paradigm.
paper_summary.summary: FUDOKI, developed by The University of Hong Kong and Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab, introduces a unified multimodal model based on discrete flow matching with kinetic optimal velocities, enabling continuous self-correction and richer bidirectional context for both visual understanding and generation. The model achieves competitive performance on various benchmarks, demonstrating improved fidelity in image generation and more robust reasoning in understanding tasks compared to prior autoregressive and mask-based approaches.

title: FUDOKI: Discrete Flow-based Unified Understanding and Generation via Kinetic-Optimal Velocities

abstract: We propose Lavida-O, a unified Masked Diffusion Model (MDM) for multimodal understanding and generation. Unlike existing multimodal MDMs such as MMaDa and Muddit which only support simple image-level understanding tasks and low-resolution image generation, Lavida-O presents a single framework that enables image-level understanding, object grounding, image editing, and high-resolution (1024px) text-to-image synthesis. Lavida-O incorporates a novel Elastic Mixture-of-Transformers (Elastic-MoT) architecture that couples a lightweight generation branch with a larger understanding branch, supported by token compression, universal text conditioning and stratified sampling for efficient and high-quality generation. Lavida-O further incorporates planning and iterative self-reflection in image generation and editing tasks, seamlessly boosting generation quality with its understanding capabilities. Lavida-O achieves state-of-the-art performance on a wide range of benchmarks including RefCOCO object grounding, GenEval text-to-image generation, and ImgEdit image editing, outperforming existing autoregressive models and continuous diffusion models such as Qwen2.5-VL and FluxKontext-dev, while offering considerable speedup at inference. These advances establish Lavida-O as a new paradigm for scalable multimodal reasoning and generation.
title: Lavida-O: Elastic Large Masked Diffusion Models for Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract:
Diffusion models have gained tremendous success in text-to-image generation,
yet still lag behind with visual understanding tasks, an area dominated by
autoregressive vision-language models. We propose a large-scale and fully
end-to-end diffusion model for multi-modal understanding and generation that
significantly improves on existing diffusion-based multimodal models, and is
the first of its kind to support the full suite of vision-language modeling
capabilities. Inspired by the multimodal diffusion transformer (MM-DiT) and
recent advances in discrete diffusion language modeling, we leverage a
cross-modal maximum likelihood estimation framework that simultaneously trains
the conditional likelihoods of both images and text jointly under a single loss
function, which is back-propagated through both branches of the diffusion
transformer. The resulting model is highly flexible and capable of a wide range
of tasks including image generation, captioning, and visual question answering.
Our model attained competitive performance compared to recent unified image
understanding and generation models, demonstrating the potential of multimodal
diffusion modeling as a promising alternative to autoregressive next-token
prediction models.

keyInsights: A single, end-to-end diffusion model can successfully perform both image generation and multimodal understanding tasks without external autoregressive components for text.
Integrating a dedicated text branch with discrete diffusion within a unified multimodal transformer significantly boosts performance on I2T tasks.
Pre-training with existing powerful text-to-image models (like SD3) and text encoders (T5) enables efficient knowledge transfer and rapid adaptation for dual-modality learning.
originalProblem: Diffusion models excelled in image generation (Text-to-Image) but largely lacked end-to-end language modeling and comprehensive image-to-text (I2T) capabilities.
Previous multimodal diffusion models often relied on external autoregressive components for text generation, preventing a truly unified diffusion framework.
A gap existed in developing empirically performant discrete diffusion processes for text tokens within a multimodal context, hindering the application of diffusion to language understanding.
results: D-DiT achieved competitive performance on various Image-to-Text benchmarks (VQAv2, VizWiz, OKVQA, GQA, POPE, MME), outperforming prior diffusion-based models and approaching autoregressive Vision-Language Models.
The model preserved and slightly improved Text-to-Image generation capabilities, achieving an FID score of 15.16 on MJHQ-30K, a notable improvement over the original SD3's 16.45.
Qualitative examples demonstrated D-DiT's ability to provide detailed and contextually appropriate responses to visual language queries, indicating fine-grained multimodal understanding.
solution: Introduced D-DiT, a large-scale Dual Diffusion Transformer with distinct but interacting image and text branches, employing cross-modal attention in every layer.
Developed a novel joint denoising objective combining continuous diffusion loss for image generation and discrete masked diffusion loss for text generation and understanding.
Utilized a multi-stage training process, including initialization with SD3-medium weights, extensive pretraining, and visual instruction tuning on diverse datasets.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from ByteDance, Carnegie Mellon, and Yale developed D-DiT, a Dual Diffusion Transformer that unifies image generation and understanding within a single, end-to-end diffusion framework.
