This model achieves competitive performance on diverse image-to-text tasks, including VQA and captioning, while also improving text-to-image generation compared to its SD3 foundation.

title: Dual Diffusion for Unified Image Generation and Understanding

abstract: Notable breakthroughs in unified understanding and generation modeling have led to remarkable advancements in image understanding, reasoning, production and editing, yet current foundational models predominantly focus on processing images, creating a gap in the development of unified models for video understanding and generation. This report presents Omni-Video, an efficient and effective unified framework for video understanding, generation, as well as instruction-based editing. Our key insight is to teach existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to produce continuous visual clues that are used as the input of diffusion decoders, which produce high-quality videos conditioned on these visual clues. To fully unlock the potential of our system for unified video modeling, we integrate several technical improvements: 1) a lightweight architectural design that respectively attaches a vision head on the top of MLLMs and a adapter before the input of diffusion decoders, the former produce visual tokens for the latter, which adapts these visual tokens to the conditional space of diffusion decoders; and 2) an efficient multi-stage training scheme that facilitates a fast connection between MLLMs and diffusion decoders with limited data and computational resources. We empirically demonstrate that our model exhibits satisfactory generalization abilities across video generation, editing and understanding tasks.

organization_info.name: Shanghai Academy of Artificial Intelligence for Science

title: Omni-Video: Democratizing Unified Video Understanding and Generation

abstract: While modern diffusion models excel at generating high-quality and diverse images, they still struggle with high-fidelity compositional and multimodal control, particularly when users simultaneously specify text prompts, subject references, spatial arrangements, pose constraints, and layout annotations. We introduce Canvas-to-Image, a unified framework that consolidates these heterogeneous controls into a single canvas interface, enabling users to generate images that faithfully reflect their intent. Our key idea is to encode diverse control signals into a single composite canvas image that the model can directly interpret for integrated visual-spatial reasoning. We further curate a suite of multi-task datasets and propose a Multi-Task Canvas Training strategy that optimizes the diffusion model to jointly understand and integrate heterogeneous controls into text-to-image generation within a unified learning paradigm. This joint training enables Canvas-to-Image to reason across multiple control modalities rather than relying on task-specific heuristics, and it generalizes well to multi-control scenarios during inference. Extensive experiments show that Canvas-to-Image significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in identity preservation and control adherence across challenging benchmarks, including multi-person composition, pose-controlled composition, layout-constrained generation, and multi-control generation.

keyInsights: Transforming heterogeneous control signals into a unified pixel-space "visual canvas" allows a single diffusion model to consistently interpret and integrate multimodal guidance without architectural changes.

A multi-task training curriculum, even when only exposed to single-control examples, enables a VLM-Diffusion model to implicitly learn the semantics of control types and generalize to novel, complex multi-control combinations during inference.

Jointly training specific layers (attention, image/text modulation) with LoRA, freezing feed-forward layers, and employing task indicator prompts is crucial for preserving pretrained quality, ensuring task disambiguation, and achieving robust identity preservation and control adherence.
originalProblem: Current generative models struggle with fine-grained, simultaneous control over multiple image aspects (e.g., subject placement, pose, layout) and often fail to coherently integrate heterogeneous control inputs.

Existing solutions typically rely on isolated control mechanisms or cumbersome stacking of multiple task-specific modules, leading to architectural complexity and poor generalization for combined controls.
The inherent stochasticity and limited control interfaces of diffusion models hinder their utility in professional creative applications requiring precise artistic intent translation.
results: Outperformed state-of-the-art baselines across single-control benchmarks (4P Composition, Pose-Overlaid 4P Composition, Layout-Guided Composition) in identity preservation (ArcFace), image quality (HPSv3), and control adherence (Control-QA).

Demonstrated superior performance in complex multi-control scenarios, seamlessly integrating identity preservation, pose guidance, and layout constraints simultaneously without artifacts, achieving the highest ArcFace, VQAScore, and Control-QA.
User studies confirmed human preference for Canvas-to-Image over baselines for both "Control Following (Pose + Box Layout)" and "Identity Preservation (Pose + Identity)" tasks.
solution: Introduces the "Multi-Task Canvas," a unified RGB input representation that consolidates diverse control modalities (spatial subject placement, pose skeletons, bounding boxes with text) into a single visual image.

Employs a Multi-Task Canvas Training strategy, jointly optimizing a VLM-Diffusion architecture (Qwen-Image-Edit) across distinct canvas types (Spatial, Pose, Box) to learn generalizable control representations.

Utilizes a task indicator prompt (e.g., "[Spatial]") to guide the model's interpretation of the canvas, enabling emergent generalization to multi-control scenarios at inference time from single-control training.
paper_summary.summary: Snap Inc. researchers developed Canvas-to-Image, a framework that unifies diverse control signals like identity, pose, and layout into a single visual canvas input for compositional image generation. This approach achieves superior fidelity and adherence to complex, simultaneous controls, outperforming baselines and demonstrating emergent generalization to unseen multi-control combinations.

title: Canvas-to-Image: Compositional Image Generation with Multimodal Controls

abstract: We present VisionLLM v2, an end-to-end generalist multimodal large model (MLLM) that unifies visual perception, understanding, and generation within a single framework. Unlike traditional MLLMs limited to text output, VisionLLM v2 significantly broadens its application scope. It excels not only in conventional visual question answering (VQA) but also in open-ended, cross-domain vision tasks such as object localization, pose estimation, and image generation and editing. To this end, we propose a new information transmission mechanism termed "super link", as a medium to connect MLLM with task-specific decoders. It not only allows flexible transmission of task information and gradient feedback between the MLLM and multiple downstream decoders but also effectively resolves training conflicts in multi-tasking scenarios. In addition, to support the diverse range of tasks, we carefully collected and combed training data from hundreds of public vision and vision-language tasks. In this way, our model can be joint-trained end-to-end on hundreds of vision language tasks and generalize to these tasks using a set of shared parameters through different user prompts, achieving performance comparable to task-specific models. We believe VisionLLM v2 will offer a new perspective on the generalization of MLLMs.

keyInsights: The "super link" mechanism, combining routing tokens and super-link queries, enables efficient, conflict-free information transfer and gradient feedback between the LLM and multiple diverse decoders.

A three-stage training strategy is essential for concurrently achieving strong conversational ability and high performance across a wide range of visual perception and generation tasks by addressing their different convergence requirements.
Separate super-link queries for each task decoder are more effective than shared queries, indicating that distinct representations are beneficial for mitigating multi-task conflicts.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are primarily limited to text-based outputs, restricting their application in tasks requiring structured visual outputs (e.g., bounding boxes, masks, keypoints) or generative visual outputs (e.g., image creation).

Prior attempts to extend MLLMs to non-text outputs either rely on external tools (hindering end-to-end optimization and gradient feedback) or struggle to scale efficiently across numerous diverse tasks due to routing complexities and training conflicts.

Jointly training a single model for a vast number of disparate vision-language tasks often leads to performance degradation or "task conflicts" where optimizing for one task negatively impacts others.
results: VisionLLM v2-Chat improved MMBench-EN/CN scores by +9.7 and +7.0 points respectively over LLaVA-NeXT-7B, demonstrating robust conversational and instruction-following abilities.
The model achieved 56.7 APb and 47.8 APm on COCO for object detection and instance segmentation, performing comparably to specialized models like Grounding-DINO-T.

VisionLLM v2 demonstrated superior multimodal in-context learning for tasks such as segmentation and image captioning compared to previous methods, and produced higher quality generated images than Stable Diffusion v1.5 with improved conditional embeddings.
solution: Introduced VisionLLM v2, an end-to-end MLLM unifying perception, understanding, and generation, by integrating a Vicuna-7B LLM with pre-trained task-specific decoders (e.g., Grounding DINO for objects/masks, UniPose for keypoints, Stable Diffusion for images).

Developed the "super link" mechanism, consisting of routing tokens predicted by the LLM and learnable super-link queries, to flexibly connect the LLM with decoders, enabling direct gradient propagation and avoiding task conflicts.

Employed a three-stage joint training strategy (multimodal training, multi-capacity fine-tuning, and decoder-only fine-tuning) on a meticulously curated, comprehensive dataset spanning hundreds of vision and vision-language tasks.
paper_summary.summary: VisionLLM v2 unifies hundreds of diverse vision and vision-language tasks within a single end-to-end generalist model, extending beyond text-only outputs to include object detection, segmentation, pose estimation, and image generation. The model introduces a "super link" mechanism to efficiently connect its central LLM with task-specific decoders, enabling high performance across various benchmarks while maintaining strong conversational abilities.

title: VisionLLM v2: An End-to-End Generalist Multimodal Large Language Model for Hundreds of Vision-Language Tasks

abstract:
We present Liquid, an auto-regressive generation paradigm that seamlessly
integrates visual comprehension and generation by tokenizing images into
discrete codes and learning these code embeddings alongside text tokens within
a shared feature space for both vision and language. Unlike previous multimodal
large language model (MLLM), Liquid achieves this integration using a single
large language model (LLM), eliminating the need for external pretrained visual
embeddings such as CLIP. For the first time, Liquid uncovers a scaling law that
performance drop unavoidably brought by the unified training of visual and
language tasks diminishes as the model size increases. Furthermore, the unified
token space enables visual generation and comprehension tasks to mutually
enhance each other, effectively removing the typical interference seen in
earlier models. We show that existing LLMs can serve as strong foundations for
Liquid, saving 100x in training costs while outperforming Chameleon in
multimodal capabilities and maintaining language performance comparable to
mainstream LLMs like LLAMA2. Liquid also outperforms models like SD v2.1 and
SD-XL (FID of 5.47 on MJHQ-30K), excelling in both vision-language and
text-only tasks. This work demonstrates that LLMs such as Qwen2.5 and GEMMA2
are powerful multimodal generators, offering a scalable solution for enhancing
both vision-language understanding and generation. The code and models will be
released at this https URL

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Visual generation performance, when integrated into LLMs via discrete tokens, adheres to power-law scaling laws, similar to those observed in pure language tasks.
The trade-off between language and visual task performance diminishes as model size increases, indicating that larger LLMs possess sufficient capacity for effective unified multimodal learning.
Visual understanding and generation tasks exhibit a mutual enhancement effect within Liquid's unified token space, where training for one modality positively reinforces the other.
originalProblem: Most existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for visual understanding rely on external visual encoders (e.g., CLIP) and are not designed for direct image generation.
MLLMs capable of visual generation typically depend on external diffusion models, leading to architectural complexity and potential scaling bottlenecks.

Prior unified models that tokenize images as discrete codes (e.g., Chameleon) are computationally expensive to train from scratch or suffer from inconsistent objectives and external pre/post-processing for visual tokens.
results: Liquid-7B achieves superior text-image alignment on GenAI-Bench, outperforming other autoregressive MLLMs and diffusion models like SD v2.1 and SD-XL.
It obtains an FID of 5.47 on MJHQ-30K for image realism, surpassing all other autoregressive models and most diffusion models, with flexible resolution generation.

The model retains undegraded linguistic capabilities, outperforming Llama2 and Chameleon in most standard text-only benchmarks, and shows improved visual understanding with semantically aligned VQVAE tokenizers.
solution: Liquid extends existing LLMs (e.g., Gemma, Qwen) by unifying image and text into a single discrete token vocabulary using a VQGAN image tokenizer, without modifying the LLM's transformer blocks.

The model undergoes continued pre-training on a hybrid multimodal dataset (text-only and image-text pairs) using a next-token prediction objective to acquire both visual understanding and generation capabilities.

A portion of text-to-image data is reversed for visual understanding training, and the models are instruction-tuned on a hybrid dataset to enhance instruction-following and overall multimodal comprehension.
paper_summary.summary: Liquid presents an efficient and scalable autoregressive paradigm that transforms existing Large Language Models into unified multimodal generators by integrating images as discrete tokens. This approach enables a single model to both understand and generate text and images, demonstrating strong performance across various multimodal benchmarks while preserving core language capabilities.

title: Liquid: Language Models are Scalable and Unified Multi-modal Generators

abstract:
A unified diffusion framework for multi-modal generation and understanding
has the transformative potential to achieve seamless and controllable image
diffusion and other cross-modal tasks. In this paper, we introduce MMGen, a
unified framework that integrates multiple generative tasks into a single
diffusion model. This includes: (1) multi-modal category-conditioned
generation, where multi-modal outputs are generated simultaneously through a
single inference process, given category information; (2) multi-modal visual
understanding, which accurately predicts depth, surface normals, and
segmentation maps from RGB images; and (3) multi-modal conditioned generation,
which produces corresponding RGB images based on specific modality conditions
and other aligned modalities. Our approach develops a novel diffusion
transformer that flexibly supports multi-modal output, along with a simple
modality-decoupling strategy to unify various tasks. Extensive experiments and
applications demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of MMGen across
diverse tasks and conditions, highlighting its potential for applications that
require simultaneous generation and understanding.
title: MMGen: Unified Multi-modal Image Generation and Understanding in One Go

abstract: We propose UniDFlow, a unified discrete flow-matching framework for multimodal understanding, generation, and editing. It decouples understanding and generation via task-specific low-rank adapters, avoiding objective interference and representation entanglement, while a novel reference-based multimodal preference alignment optimizes relative outcomes under identical conditioning, improving faithfulness and controllability without large-scale retraining. UniDFlpw achieves SOTA performance across eight benchmarks and exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to tasks including inpainting, in-context image generation, reference-based editing, and compositional generation, despite no explicit task-specific training.

keyInsights: Decoupling understanding and generation capabilities using task-specific LoRA adapters prevents objective interference and parameter entanglement, allowing independent improvement without degradation.

The Time-Step Guided RMSNorm (TSG-RMSNorm) is crucial for stable training, preserving pretrained feature distributions while injecting time information, thereby maintaining the VLM's inherent reasoning priors.

Reference-based Multimodal Preference Alignment (mRef-DPO) effectively addresses the limitations of likelihood-based training by optimizing explicit relative preferences, leading to demonstrably more faithful and controllable multimodal outputs.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI systems operate disjointly, with LLMs excelling in reasoning but lacking visual generation, and diffusion models providing high-fidelity generation without deep semantic understanding.

Prior attempts like autoregressive transformers (e.g., Emu) are highly inefficient for high-dimensional visual outputs, while hybrid models (e.g., EMMA) face issues with mismatched objectives, high computational costs, and parameter entanglement.

Generative and editing capabilities in previous models often lacked precise control and faithfulness to instructions, as alignment methods optimized outputs in isolation rather than modeling relative preferences under identical conditions.
results: Achieved significant performance gains in multimodal understanding, outperforming leading understanding-only models like Qwen2.5-VL (7B) by 20.4% overall on EvalVLM and scoring 63.8 on OCRBenchV2, surpassing MammothModa2 by 7.7 points.

Demonstrated superior text-to-image generation, achieving an overall score of 0.95 on GenEval (+2.2% over EMMA) and outperforming larger, generation-focused models like Qwen-Image (7B+20B) by 4.0% on GenEval.

Exhibited precise instruction-guided image editing, surpassing EMMA by +3.5% on Emu-Edit and achieving an overall score of 4.24 on ImgEdit Bench, with strong performance in reasoning-driven editing tasks.
solution: Developed UniDFlow, a unified discrete flow-matching (DFM) framework that casts multimodal understanding, generation, and editing as a single discrete denoising process.

Employed parameter-efficient Low-Rank Adapters (LoRA) for all task-specific adaptations on a frozen, pretrained vision-language transformer backbone, preserving original reasoning priors and reducing computational costs.

Introduced a three-stage training pipeline, including text-focused alignment, vision-focused alignment, and a novel reference-based multimodal preference alignment (mRef-DPO) with Mixture-of-LoRA Routing (MoRA), to decouple capabilities and enhance controllability.
paper_summary.summary: UniDFlow unifies multimodal understanding, generation, and editing within a single discrete flow-matching framework, employing parameter-efficient adapters and a novel reference-based preference alignment strategy. The model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across various benchmarks for multimodal understanding, text-to-image generation, and instruction-guided image editing, while preserving strong reasoning capabilities.

title: Best of Both Worlds: Multimodal Reasoning and Generation via Unified Discrete Flow Matching

abstract: Current unified multimodal models typically rely on discrete visual tokenizers to bridge the modality gap. However, discretization inevitably discards fine-grained semantic information, leading to suboptimal performance in visual understanding tasks. Conversely, directly modeling continuous semantic representations (e.g., CLIP, SigLIP) poses significant challenges in high-dimensional generative modeling, resulting in slow convergence and training instability. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce UniCom, a unified framework that harmonizes multimodal understanding and generation via compressed continuous representation. We empirically demonstrate that reducing channel dimension is significantly more effective than spatial downsampling for both reconstruction and generation. Accordingly, we design an attention-based semantic compressor to distill dense features into a compact unified representation. Furthermore, we validate that the transfusion architecture surpasses query-based designs in convergence and consistency. Experiments demonstrate that UniCom achieves state-of-the-art generation performance among unified models. Notably, by preserving rich semantic priors, it delivers exceptional controllability in image editing and maintains image consistency even without relying on VAE.

keyInsights: Channel-wise compression of continuous visual features is more effective than sequence length reduction for preserving fine-grained details and accelerating generative model convergence.
An attention-based projector in the Semantic Compressor is crucial for maintaining structured semantic information from visual inputs, outperforming simpler MLP designs.
Pre-training with visual-language alignment from VLMs significantly bootstraps and enhances the generative process, leading to faster convergence and higher performance.
originalProblem: Existing unified multimodal models often lose fine-grained semantic information during discrete tokenization of visual data.
Directly modeling high-dimensional continuous visual features for generation is challenging due to complex, non-smooth data distributions, leading to slow convergence and instability.
There is a dilemma in designing unified multimodal models that can both understand and generate content with high fidelity across modalities.
results: Achieved negligible reconstruction loss with an 18x channel dimension reduction (rFID 0.42 for d64 vs. 0.38 for d1152), effectively recovering high-frequency details like small text and facial identity.
Demonstrated competitive performance on text-to-image generation benchmarks (GenEval, DPG-Bench, WISE) and secured leading scores on image editing benchmarks (ImgEdit-Bench, GEdit-Bench).
Maintained high image consistency in editing tasks without relying on VAE latents for identity preservation, conditioning solely on text and reference image semantic features.
solution: UniCom proposes a two-stage generative process utilizing a Semantic Compressor (C_ϕ), a Generative Prior Module, and a Diffusion Decoder.
The Semantic Compressor uses a lightweight, attention-based Transformer to project high-dimensional VLM features into a compact, continuous latent space, optimizing for channel dimension reduction.
A Generative Prior Module, specifically a Transfusion-inspired unified Transformer, learns to predict these compressed continuous representations from text conditions.
paper_summary.summary: The UniCom framework unifies multimodal understanding and generation by introducing compressed continuous semantic representations, achieving high-fidelity image reconstruction and competitive performance in text-to-image generation and image editing tasks. This method effectively bridges the gap between preserving fine-grained details and ensuring generative tractability by employing an attention-based channel-wise compression strategy.

title: UniCom: Unified Multimodal Modeling via Compressed Continuous Semantic Representations

abstract: We present UniModel, a unified generative model that jointly supports visual understanding and visual generation within a single pixel-to-pixel diffusion framework. Our goal is to achieve unification along three axes: the model, the tasks, and the representations. At the representation level, we eliminate modality discrepancies by mapping both text and images into a shared visual space: textual prompts are rendered as painted text images on a clean canvas, and all inputs and outputs are treated purely as RGB pixels. This yields a fully vision-native formulation of multimodal learning. At the task level, a broad range of vision-language problems are cast as pixel-to-pixel transformations in this visual space. For understanding tasks, the model takes an RGB image and produces a painted text image that visually encodes the semantic prediction. For generation tasks, painted text images serve as visual conditions that guide realistic and semantically aligned image synthesis. Captioning and text-to-image generation thus become different directions of the same underlying visual translation process. At the model level, we instantiate a single Unified Diffusion Transformer trained with rectified flow in pixel space. A shared backbone jointly learns bidirectional mappings between natural images and painted text images, with lightweight task embeddings to specify the desired direction. Experiments on text-to-image synthesis and image-to-text understanding demonstrate strong cross-modal alignment and emergent controllability such as cycle-consistent image-caption-image loops. Our initial exploration suggests that unifying model, tasks, and representations in a single visual space is a promising paradigm for general-purpose multimodal intelligence.

organization_info.name: Institute of Artificial Intelligence (TeleAI), China Telecom

keyInsights: Representing text as visual data (painted text images) enables fundamental unification of multimodal learning at the representation, task, and model levels.
A single diffusion transformer, operating solely in pixel space, can jointly learn both visual understanding (image-to-text) and generation (text-to-image) tasks with a consistent objective.
The shared visual space and bidirectional training naturally foster strong cycle consistency and emergent visual controllability, allowing text edits to intuitively influence image generation.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal models often achieve only partial unification, relying on heterogeneous representations (e.g., separate encoders for text and images) and fragmented task formulations.
These models typically treat 'image    text' and 'text    image' as distinct problems, leading to suboptimal cross-modal alignment and limited bidirectional control.
Current systems struggle to achieve a truly symmetric and cohesive understanding of image-text relationships within a single, consistent framework.
results: For text-to-image generation, UniModel produces visually compelling and semantically faithful images from painted text prompts, robust to variations in text layout and font.
In image understanding tasks, the model generates semantically accurate and spatially well-structured painted text outputs, implicitly learning language semantics and text layout conventions.
UniModel naturally supports cycle-consistent inference (RGB    painted text    reconstructed RGB), preserving semantics and visual attributes without explicit cycle-consistency loss.
Training efficiency is identified as a limitation, requiring longer convergence times, and generated painted text images can sometimes exhibit visual artifacts or spelling inconsistencies.
solution: UniModel converts all textual content (prompts, captions) into 'painted text images' on a canvas, ensuring both natural RGB images and text share the same pixel-level representation.
It employs a unified bidirectional diffusion transformer architecture, based on MMDiT, that operates entirely within a shared visual latent space.

The model is trained using a rectified-flow objective with bidirectional training, where paired RGB and painted-text latents are randomly assigned as source or target, guided by lightweight task embeddings.
paper_summary.summary: UniModel introduces a visual-only framework that unifies multimodal understanding and generation by representing both text and images as pixel-level data within a single diffusion transformer. This approach enables coherent text-to-image generation and image captioning, demonstrating strong cycle consistency and emergent controllability by operating entirely in a shared visual latent space.

title: UniModel: A Visual-Only Framework for Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract: Unifying diverse image generation tasks within a single framework remains a fundamental challenge in visual generation. While large language models (LLMs) achieve unification through task-agnostic data and generation, existing visual generation models fail to meet these principles. Current approaches either rely on per-task datasets and large-scale training or adapt pre-trained image models with task-specific modifications, limiting their generalizability. In this work, we explore video models as a foundation for unified image generation, leveraging their inherent ability to model temporal correlations. We introduce RealGeneral, a novel framework that reformulates image generation as a conditional frame prediction task, analogous to in-context learning in LLMs. To bridge the gap between video models and condition-image pairs, we propose (1) a Unified Conditional Embedding module for multi-modal alignment and (2) a Unified Stream DiT Block with decoupled adaptive LayerNorm and attention mask to mitigate cross-modal interference. RealGeneral demonstrates effectiveness in multiple important visual generation tasks, e.g., it achieves a 14.5% improvement in subject similarity for customized generation and a 10% enhancement in image quality for canny-to-image task. Project page: this https URL GitHub Link: this https URL

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: RealGeneral: Unifying Visual Generation via Temporal In-Context Learning with Video Models

abstract: Recent diffusion and flow matching models have demonstrated strong capabilities in image generation and editing by progressively removing noise through iterative sampling. While this enables flexible inversion for semantic-preserving edits, few-step sampling regimes suffer from poor forward process approximation, leading to degraded editing quality. Existing few-step inversion methods often rely on pretrained generators and auxiliary modules, limiting scalability and generalization across different architectures. To address these limitations, we propose BiFM (Bidirectional Flow Matching), a unified framework that jointly learns generation and inversion within a single model. BiFM directly estimates average velocity fields in both ``image $\to$ noise" and ``noise $\to$ image" directions, constrained by a shared instantaneous velocity field derived from either predefined schedules or pretrained multi-step diffusion models. Additionally, BiFM introduces a novel training strategy using continuous time-interval supervision, stabilized by a bidirectional consistency objective and a lightweight time-interval embedding. This bidirectional formulation also enables one-step inversion and can integrate seamlessly into popular diffusion and flow matching backbones. Across diverse image editing and generation tasks, BiFM consistently outperforms existing few-step approaches, achieving superior performance and editability.

keyInsights: The MeanFlow Identity can be extended bidirectionally to consistently define average velocity fields for both generation and inversion, allowing a single model to learn both processes.
Directly learning average velocity fields over continuous time intervals, rather than instantaneous velocities, provides a robust and efficient mechanism for few-step ODE integration.

A specific training strategy, involving continuous time-interval supervision, a weighted bidirectional consistency loss, and a lightweight time-interval embedding, is essential for stable and high-quality bidirectional learning.
originalProblem: Few-step generative models struggle with accurate image inversion, leading to approximation errors like semantic drift or poor background preservation during editing.
Existing training-free inversion methods are prone to significant errors with large time steps, while tuning-based methods incur high computational overhead and complexity.
A trade-off exists between sampling speed and quality, particularly in few-step regimes, where rapid inference often compromises the fidelity of image generation and editing.
results: BiFM achieved superior image reconstruction, demonstrating better background preservation (e.g., lower LPIPS, higher SSIM, PSNR) and sharper details compared to existing inversion baselines on PIE-Bench.
For few-step prompt-based image editing (4 NFEs), BiFM outperformed training-free (DDIM, ReNoise) and auxiliary network (TurboEdit) methods in LPIPS (67.25), SSIM (87.29), and PSNR (28.92).

In image generation, BiFM reduced the FID score on MSCOCO-256 to 4.57 (from 6.05 for vanilla MMDiT) and improved one-step generation on CIFAR-10 to an FID of 2.75, surpassing sCT (2.85) and MeanFlow (2.92).
solution: BiFM introduces a unified framework that jointly learns few-step image generation and inversion through bidirectional flow matching.
The model is parameterized to directly estimate average velocity fields for both forward (generation) and backward (inversion) time directions, based on an extended MeanFlow Identity.

A bidirectional consistency objective is incorporated into the training, explicitly enforcing reversibility between the learned forward and backward average velocity predictions to stabilize few-step inversion.
paper_summary.summary: BiFM presents a unified framework for efficient few-step image editing and generation by extending flow matching to learn bidirectional average velocity fields. This approach consistently improves image reconstruction, enhances prompt-based editing fidelity, and reduces FID scores in generation tasks across various datasets, such as achieving an FID of 4.57 on MSCOCO-256, outperforming vanilla MMDiT's 6.05.

title: BiFM: Bidirectional Flow Matching for Few-Step Image Editing and Generation

abstract: Unified vision large language models (VLLMs) have recently achieved impressive advancements in both multimodal understanding and generation, powering applications such as visual question answering and text-guided image synthesis. However, progress in unified VLLMs remains constrained by the lack of datasets that fully exploit the synergistic potential between these two core abilities. Existing datasets typically address understanding and generation in isolation, thereby limiting the performance of unified VLLMs. To bridge this critical gap, we introduce a novel dataset construction framework, UnifiedVisual, and present UnifiedVisual-240K, a high-quality dataset meticulously designed to facilitate mutual enhancement between multimodal understanding and generation. UnifiedVisual-240K seamlessly integrates diverse visual and textual inputs and outputs, enabling comprehensive cross-modal reasoning and precise text-to-image alignment. Our dataset encompasses a wide spectrum of tasks and data sources, ensuring rich diversity and addressing key shortcomings of prior resources. Extensive experiments demonstrate that models trained on UnifiedVisual-240K consistently achieve strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Notably, these models exhibit significant mutual reinforcement between multimodal understanding and generation, further validating the effectiveness of our framework and dataset. We believe UnifiedVisual represents a new growth point for advancing unified VLLMs and unlocking their full potential. Our code and datasets is available at this https URL.
title: UnifiedVisual: A Framework for Constructing Unified Vision-Language Datasets

abstract:
Diffusion models, and their generalization, flow matching, have had a
remarkable impact on the field of media generation. Here, the conventional
approach is to learn the complex mapping from a simple source distribution of
Gaussian noise to the target media distribution. For cross-modal tasks such as
text-to-image generation, this same mapping from noise to image is learnt
whilst including a conditioning mechanism in the model. One key and thus far
relatively unexplored feature of flow matching is that, unlike Diffusion
models, they are not constrained for the source distribution to be noise.
Hence, in this paper, we propose a paradigm shift, and ask the question of
whether we can instead train flow matching models to learn a direct mapping
from the distribution of one modality to the distribution of another, thus
obviating the need for both the noise distribution and conditioning mechanism.
We present a general and simple framework, CrossFlow, for cross-modal flow
matching. We show the importance of applying Variational Encoders to the input
data, and introduce a method to enable Classifier-free guidance. Surprisingly,
for text-to-image, CrossFlow with a vanilla transformer without cross attention
slightly outperforms standard flow matching, and we show that it scales better
with training steps and model size, while also allowing for interesting latent
arithmetic which results in semantically meaningful edits in the output space.
To demonstrate the generalizability of our approach, we also show that
CrossFlow is on par with or outperforms the state-of-the-art for various
cross-modal / intra-modal mapping tasks, viz. image captioning, depth
estimation, and image super-resolution. We hope this paper contributes to
accelerating progress in cross-modal media generation.

keyInsights: Generative models can effectively learn direct mappings between correlated cross-modal latent spaces, potentially eliminating the need for Gaussian noise and explicit conditioning mechanisms like cross-attention.
A regularized, semantically rich source latent space, often achieved through a Variational Encoder with a contrastive loss, is crucial for high-quality direct cross-modal evolution.
A unified, simpler architecture (vanilla Transformer) can generalize across diverse cross-modal and intra-modal tasks without task-specific modifications.
originalProblem: Conventional generative models typically start from Gaussian noise and rely on explicit conditioning mechanisms (e.g., cross-attention) for cross-modal generation.
Directly mapping between modalities with different data shapes and distributions (e.g., text and image) poses challenges for existing flow matching models.
Implementing Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG), which is crucial for generation quality, is difficult in a framework that aims to remove explicit conditioning inputs.
results: Achieves a zero-shot FID-30K of 10.13 for text-to-image generation, slightly outperforming standard flow matching baselines that use cross-attention.
Demonstrates successful latent arithmetic operations in the text latent space, enabling semantically coherent image edits with a 91.4% success rate.
Successfully applies the unified framework to image captioning (achieving competitive performance on COCO Karpathy split), monocular depth estimation, and image super-resolution.
solution: Introduces CrossFlow, a framework that trains a flow matching model to directly evolve a source latent representation (from an input modality) into a target latent representation (for an output modality).
Employs a Variational Encoder (VE) to convert input modalities into a regularized, shape-aligned latent space, using a contrastive loss for semantic alignment in text-to-image tasks.
Enables Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) through a binary conditioning indicator, allowing the model to learn conditional and unconditional generation paths without explicit conditioning inputs.
paper_summary.summary: CrossFlow introduces a noise-free framework for cross-modal media generation by directly evolving one modality's latent representation into another's, bypassing the need for Gaussian noise or explicit cross-attention. It achieves competitive performance in text-to-image generation, outperforming standard flow matching baselines, and generalizes effectively across tasks like image captioning and depth estimation.

title: Flowing from Words to Pixels: A Noise-Free Framework for Cross-Modality  Evolution

abstract: We present UniMotion, to our knowledge the first unified framework for simultaneous understanding and generation of human motion, natural language, and RGB images within a single architecture. Existing unified models handle only restricted modality subsets (e.g., Motion-Text or static Pose-Image) and predominantly rely on discrete tokenization, which introduces quantization errors and disrupts temporal continuity. UniMotion overcomes both limitations through a core principle: treating motion as a first-class continuous modality on equal footing with RGB. A novel Cross-Modal Aligned Motion VAE (CMA-VAE) and symmetric dual-path embedders construct parallel continuous pathways for Motion and RGB within a shared LLM backbone. To inject visual-semantic priors into motion representations without requiring images at inference, we propose Dual-Posterior KL Alignment (DPA), which distills a vision-fused encoder's richer posterior into the motion-only encoder. To address the cold-start problem -- where text supervision alone is too sparse to calibrate the newly introduced motion pathway -- we further propose Latent Reconstruction Alignment (LRA), a self-supervised pre-training strategy that uses dense motion latents as unambiguous conditions to co-calibrate the embedder, backbone, and flow head, establishing a stable motion-aware foundation for all downstream tasks. UniMotion achieves state-of-the-art performance across seven tasks spanning any-to-any understanding, generation, and editing among the three modalities, with especially strong advantages on cross-modal compositional tasks.

keyInsights: Continuous motion representation, as achieved by CMA-VAE, significantly outperforms discrete tokenization by preserving kinematic nuances, reducing artifacts, and improving downstream task performance.

Dual-Posterior KL Alignment (DPA) effectively injects visual-semantic priors into motion representations during training, enhancing their quality for cross-modal tasks without needing images during inference.

Latent Reconstruction Alignment (LRA) is critical for providing dense geometric supervision to co-calibrate the motion generation pathway, establishing a robust foundation for learning from sparse text.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI models typically only integrate subsets of modalities (e.g., Motion-Text, Vision-Text) or static pose, failing to unify continuous human motion, natural language, and RGB images within a single architecture.
Previous motion/pose representations often rely on discrete tokenization, which introduces quantization errors, degrades temporal continuity, and complicates cross-modal alignment.
Calibrating motion generation from sparse textual supervision presents a "cold-start" challenge, as dense, kinematically rich motion needs to be generated effectively.
results: UniMotion demonstrates state-of-the-art or leading performance across seven diverse tri-modal understanding, generation, and editing tasks, including Text-to-Motion (R@3 0.841) and Motion-to-Text (BertScore 41.2).
Its continuous motion representation (CMA-VAE) significantly improves reconstruction quality, achieving an Average Position Error (APE) of 3.53cm, substantially better than discrete VQ-VAE's 17.15cm.
The framework is the first unified motion-aware MLLM to support end-to-end motion-guided image editing, outperforming multi-stage baselines with an FID of 18.92 and a CLIP score of 0.31.
solution: Introduces UniMotion, a unified framework that treats human motion as a first-class continuous modality, integrating it symmetrically with natural language and RGB images.
Employs a Cross-Modal Aligned Motion VAE (CMA-VAE) with Dual-Posterior KL Alignment (DPA) to encode continuous motion and implicitly absorb visual-semantic supervision during training.

Utilizes Latent Reconstruction Alignment (LRA), a self-supervised strategy, to pre-train and calibrate the motion pathway using dense motion prompts, addressing the "cold-start" problem from sparse text.
paper_summary.summary: UniMotion introduces a unified framework for simultaneously understanding, generating, and editing across human motion, natural language, and RGB images. It integrates continuous motion representations and achieves leading performance on seven tri-modal tasks, outperforming existing specialized and partial unified models.

title: UniMotion: A Unified Framework for Motion-Text-Vision Understanding and Generation

abstract:
Recent advances in multimodal foundation models unifying image understanding
and generation have opened exciting avenues for tackling a wide range of
vision-language tasks within a single framework. Despite progress, existing
unified models typically require extensive pretraining and struggle to achieve
the same level of performance compared to models dedicated to each task.
Additionally, many of these models suffer from slow image generation speeds,
limiting their practical deployment in real-time or resource-constrained
settings. In this work, we propose Layerwise Timestep-Expert Flow-based
Transformer (LaTtE-Flow), a novel and efficient architecture that unifies image
understanding and generation within a single multimodal model. LaTtE-Flow
builds upon powerful pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to inherit strong
multimodal understanding capabilities, and extends them with a novel Layerwise
Timestep Experts flow-based architecture for efficient image generation.
LaTtE-Flow distributes the flow-matching process across specialized groups of
Transformer layers, each responsible for a distinct subset of timesteps. This
design significantly improves sampling efficiency by activating only a small
subset of layers at each sampling timestep. To further enhance performance, we
propose a Timestep-Conditioned Residual Attention mechanism for efficient
information reuse across layers. Experiments demonstrate that LaTtE-Flow
achieves strong performance on multimodal understanding tasks, while achieving
competitive image generation quality with around 6x faster inference speed
compared to recent unified multimodal models.

keyInsights: Layer-wise partitioning of timestep processing dramatically reduces computational overhead while maintaining quality, as different denoising stages benefit from specialized layer groups
Timestep-conditioned residual connections enable intelligent information reuse across layers, with attention patterns evolving dynamically based on generation stage
Frozen VLM integration allows unified models to inherit robust understanding without sacrificing generation efficiency or requiring expensive joint training
Conflicting gradients across timesteps can be mitigated through expert specialization, leading to faster convergence and better final performance
originalProblem: Existing unified multimodal models face severe computational inefficiency during image generation, requiring dozens of full-model forward passes through all layers at each timestep
Current approaches suffer from conflicting optimization gradients across different timesteps in flow-matching, leading to slow training convergence
Unified models typically exhibit performance trade-offs where improvements in generation come at the expense of understanding capabilities or vice versa
Slow inference speeds (6-48× slower than needed) make these models impractical for real-time applications and resource-constrained deployment
results: Achieves 6× faster inference than Janus Pro and 48× speedup over Show-o while using only 500M activated parameters per step versus 1.5B for baselines
Demonstrates competitive ImageNet generation quality with FID 5.79 outperforming Show-o (31.26) and Janus Pro (23.68) across multiple metrics
Maintains strong multimodal understanding with highest scores on MM-Vet (51.5) and MMMUR (1501.4) benchmarks compared to other unified models
Shows significantly faster training convergence compared to vanilla baselines, with ablation studies confirming the contribution of each architectural component
solution: Introduce Layerwise Timestep Experts that partition transformer layers into non-overlapping groups, with each group specializing in specific timestep ranges during flow-matching
Implement Timestep-Conditioned Residual Attention that uses learned gating vectors to dynamically modulate information reuse from previous attention layers based on current timestep
Leverage frozen pretrained VLM backbone (Qwen2-VL-2B) to inherit strong understanding capabilities while adding efficient generative pathways
Design two architectural variants (Couple and Blend) that balance parameter sharing and task specialization for optimal performance
paper_summary.summary: University of Illinois and Virginia Tech researchers in collaboration with Nvidia and Intuit AI Research develop LaTtE-Flow, a unified multimodal model that achieves 6× faster image generation inference than Janus Pro and 48× speedup over Show-o while maintaining competitive multimodal understanding performance, through a novel Layerwise Timestep Experts architecture that partitions the 28-layer Qwen2-VL backbone into 4 groups of 7 layers each, where only one group activates per timestep during flow-matching generation (reducing computational cost from O(L×T) to O(M×T) where M<<L), combined with Timestep-Conditioned Residual Attention that dynamically gates information reuse across layers using learned timestep embeddings, enabling the model to leverage frozen VLM understanding capabilities while achieving competitive ImageNet generation quality (FID 5.79) and superior multimodal benchmark scores including 51.5 on MM-Vet through efficient integration of flow-matching generative modeling with pretrained vision-language foundations.

title: LaTtE-Flow: Layerwise Timestep-Expert Flow-based Transformer

abstract: Diffusion models (DMs) excel in photorealism, image editing, and solving inverse problems, aided by classifier-free guidance and image inversion techniques. However, rectified flow models (RFMs) remain underexplored for these tasks. Existing DM-based methods often require additional training, lack generalization to pretrained latent models, underperform, and demand significant computational resources due to extensive backpropagation through ODE solvers and inversion processes. In this work, we first develop a theoretical and empirical understanding of the vector field dynamics of RFMs in efficiently guiding the denoising trajectory. Our findings reveal that we can navigate the vector field in a deterministic and gradient-free manner. Utilizing this property, we propose FlowChef, which leverages the vector field to steer the denoising trajectory for controlled image generation tasks, facilitated by gradient skipping. FlowChef is a unified framework for controlled image generation that, for the first time, simultaneously addresses classifier guidance, linear inverse problems, and image editing without the need for extra training, inversion, or intensive backpropagation. Finally, we perform extensive evaluations and show that FlowChef significantly outperforms baselines in terms of performance, memory, and time requirements, achieving new state-of-the-art results. Project Page: \url{this https URL}.
title: Steering Rectified Flow Models in the Vector Field for Controlled Image Generation

abstract: Despite advancements in generating visually stunning content, video diffusion models (VDMs) often yield physically inconsistent results due to pixel-only reconstruction. To address this, we propose MMPhysVideo, the first framework to scale physical plausibility in video generation through joint multimodal modeling. We recast perceptual cues, specifically semantics, geometry, and spatio-temporal trajectory, into a unified pseudo-RGB format, enabling VDMs to directly capture complex physical dynamics. To mitigate cross-modal interference, we propose a Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher architecture, which utilizes parallel branches to fully decouple RGB and perception processing and adopts two zero-initialized control links to gradually learn pixel-wise consistency. For inference efficiency, the teacher's physical prior is distilled into a single-stream student model via representation alignment. Furthermore, we present MMPhysPipe, a scalable data curation and annotation pipeline tailored for constructing physics-rich multimodal datasets. MMPhysPipe employs a vision-language model (VLM) guided by a chain-of-visual-evidence rule to pinpoint physical subjects, enabling expert models to extract multi-granular perceptual information. Without additional inference costs, MMPhysVideo consistently improves physical plausibility and visual quality over advanced models across various benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating multi-granular perceptual cues through a pseudo-RGB format enables generative models to internalize physical dynamics more effectively than implicit or textual methods.

A dual-stream architecture with parallel branches and zero-initialized bidirectional control links effectively processes RGB and perceptual modalities concurrently while mitigating inter-modal interference.

Distilling knowledge from a complex dual-stream teacher model into an efficient single-stream student via relation-based representation alignment enables physically plausible generation without additional inference overhead.
originalProblem: Current video diffusion models often produce visually appealing but physically inconsistent videos, limiting their utility in applications requiring realistic simulations.

Existing methods for integrating physical priors (e.g., textual prompts, implicit knowledge transfer) suffer from domain gaps, indirect physical connections, or high training costs and potential reward hacking.
A lack of scalable, physics-rich multimodal datasets with corresponding multi-granular perceptual annotations hinders the training of models to inherently understand physical dynamics.
results: MMPhysVideo consistently improved Average Physical Consistency (PC) scores on physics benchmarks (VideoPhy and PhyGenBench), achieving state-of-the-art results, such as a 5.0% increase on VideoPhy and 6.2% on PhyGenBench with the CogVideoX-5B backbone.

Qualitative evaluations demonstrated superior physical plausibility in generated videos compared to baselines, showing more accurate fine-grained dynamics, improved spatial relationships, and better structural integrity of interacting objects.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of the Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher architecture, the unified multimodal perceptual cues, each component of the MMPhysPipe data pipeline, and the representation alignment distillation strategy.
solution: MMPhysVideo employs a two-stage training framework: Stage I trains a Bidirectionally Controlled Teacher (BCT) model for joint RGB-perceptual modeling, and Stage II distills this knowledge into an efficient single-stream student model.
Various perceptual cues (semantics, geometry, spatio-temporal trajectory) are recast into a unified pseudo-RGB video representation, making them directly compatible with video diffusion models.

The MMPhysPipe pipeline automatically curates physics-rich videos and generates multi-granular annotations, including instance segmentation and 3D point tracking, to create the MMPhysData-36k dataset.
paper_summary.summary: MMPhysVideo introduces a framework that enhances the physical plausibility of generated videos by integrating multi-granular perceptual cues, such such as semantics, geometry, and spatio-temporal trajectory, directly into video diffusion models. This approach, supported by a novel dual-stream architecture and a specialized data pipeline, leads to videos with improved physical consistency without compromising visual quality.

title: MMPhysVideo: Scaling Physical Plausibility in Video Generation via Joint Multimodal Modeling

abstract: Integrating image generation and understanding into a single framework has become a pivotal goal in the multimodal domain. However, how understanding can effectively assist generation has not been fully explored. Unlike previous works that focus on leveraging reasoning abilities and world knowledge from understanding models, this paper introduces a novel perspective: leveraging understanding to enhance the fidelity and detail richness of generated images. To this end, we propose Forge-and-Quench, a new unified framework that puts this principle into practice. In the generation process of our framework, an MLLM first reasons over the entire conversational context, including text instructions, to produce an enhanced text instruction. This refined instruction is then mapped to a virtual visual representation, termed the Bridge Feature, via a novel Bridge Adapter. This feature acts as a crucial link, forging insights from the understanding model to quench and refine the generation process. It is subsequently injected into the T2I backbone as a visual guidance signal, alongside the enhanced text instruction that replaces the original input. To validate this paradigm, we conduct comprehensive studies on the design of the Bridge Feature and Bridge Adapter. Our framework demonstrates exceptional extensibility and flexibility, enabling efficient migration across different MLLM and T2I models with significant savings in training overhead, all without compromising the MLLM's inherent multimodal understanding capabilities. Experiments show that Forge-and-Quench significantly improves image fidelity and detail across multiple models, while also maintaining instruction-following accuracy and enhancing world knowledge application. Models and codes are available at this https URL.

keyInsights: An MLLM can be trained to "forge" a virtual visual signal from text alone, providing explicit, fine-grained visual conditioning that emulates the fidelity-enhancing effects of real reference images.

Dual-path conditioning, leveraging both MLLM-enhanced semantic text and a forged virtual visual feature, effectively overcomes the informational bottleneck in MLLM-T2I integration, leading to richer image details.

The choice of visual encoder for the target feature space is critical, requiring a balance between high fidelity and robustness to approximation errors inherent in the text-driven forging process, with SigLIP-ViT being the optimal choice.
originalProblem: Unified multimodal models, particularly adapter-based MLLM-T2I integrations, create an "informational bottleneck" preventing the MLLM's rich visual understanding from fully translating into fine-grained image details.

Current methods primarily rely on MLLM-enhanced text prompts or single semantic embeddings, leading to a loss of nuanced visual information like texture, lighting, and composition during image generation.
Achieving high-fidelity image generation often requires real reference images, which are typically unavailable in standard text-to-image generation tasks.
results: The framework achieved substantial visual quality improvements, reducing COCO-30K FID for MeiGen-Image from 23.97 to 19.86 and for FLUX.1-dev from 27.71 to 20.83.

Human evaluations showed strong preference for Forge-and-Quench outputs, with MeiGen-Image-FaQ winning 71.0% of visual quality comparisons, alongside improved world knowledge application (e.g., MeiGen-Image WISE score from 0.55 to 0.70) without compromising prompt alignment.
Qualitative analysis demonstrated noticeably enhanced realism, finer textures, richer detail, and a significant reduction in common AI artifacts in generated images across various prompts.
solution: The "Forge" stage uses an MLLM to semantically enrich the initial text prompt and simultaneously generate a high-quality "Bridge Feature," a virtual visual signal mimicking real image embeddings.
The Bridge Feature is created by training a diffusion-based Bridge Adapter to map the MLLM's abstract embedding to a target SigLIP visual feature space.

The "Quench" stage injects both the enhanced text prompt and the forged Bridge Feature into a frozen T2I backbone via a lightweight Injection Adapter, guiding the denoising process with dual conditioning.
paper_summary.summary: Meituan's Forge-and-Quench framework enhances image generation fidelity by having a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) generate a "virtual visual feature" from text instructions. This virtual signal, combined with an enhanced text prompt, guides a frozen Text-to-Image (T2I) model, leading to improved visual quality quantified by a COCO-30K FID reduction from 23.97 to 19.86 for the MeiGen-Image backbone.

title: Forge-and-Quench: Enhancing Image Generation for Higher Fidelity in Unified Multimodal Models

abstract: The remarkable success of diffusion models in text-to-image generation has sparked growing interest in expanding their capabilities to a variety of multi-modal tasks, including image understanding, manipulation, and perception. These tasks require advanced semantic comprehension across both visual and textual modalities, especially in scenarios involving complex semantic instructions. However, existing approaches often rely heavily on vision-language models (VLMs) or modular designs for semantic guidance, leading to fragmented architectures and computational inefficiency. To address these challenges, we propose UniAlignment, a unified multimodal generation framework within a single diffusion transformer. UniAlignment introduces a dual-stream diffusion training strategy that incorporates both intrinsic-modal semantic alignment and cross-modal semantic alignment, thereby enhancing the model's cross-modal consistency and instruction-following robustness. Additionally, we present SemGen-Bench, a new benchmark specifically designed to evaluate multimodal semantic consistency under complex textual instructions. Extensive experiments across multiple tasks and benchmarks demonstrate that UniAlignment outperforms existing baselines, underscoring the significant potential of diffusion models in unified multimodal generation.

keyInsights: A single Diffusion Transformer (DiT) can effectively unify diverse multimodal tasks by sharing parameters across continuous and discrete diffusion processes.

Specific semantic alignment strategies, both cross-modal (contrastive) and intrinsic-modal (VLM-guided latent regularization), are crucial for enhancing instruction following and semantic grounding in unified diffusion models without adding inference-time complexity.
A progressive multi-stage training approach is essential for balancing learning across heterogeneous multimodal tasks and preventing catastrophic forgetting.

Rigorous evaluation with complex, compositional instructions, as provided by SemGen-Bench, is critical for accurately assessing the semantic fidelity and multimodal alignment capabilities of unified generative models.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal AI models often rely on fragmented architectures, leading to computational inefficiency, redundant parameters, and limited generalizability across diverse tasks.
Diffusion models, while excelling in image fidelity, typically lack robust semantic comprehension and struggle to follow complex, compositional textual instructions for generation and manipulation.
Current benchmarks for unified generative models often employ simplistic semantics and rigid instruction formats, failing to rigorously evaluate true multimodal semantic consistency.
results: UniAlignment achieves top-2 performance across multiple standard benchmarks for text-to-image generation (e.g., highest on DPG-Bench with 85.64) and image editing (e.g., second on GEdit-Bench-EN, with the highest Semantic Consistency score of 7.25).

On the novel SemGen-Bench, the model ranks top-2 across nearly all challenging categories (T2I-Long, T2I-Sem, Edit-Multi, Edit-Sem), demonstrating strong semantic robustness under complex instructions.

The model, with 2 billion parameters, exhibits significantly faster generation times (2s per image) compared to other unified models (e.g., UniWorld 21s, OmniGen2 37s), showcasing its computational efficiency.
Ablation studies confirm that cross-modal alignment improves instruction consistency and CLIP scores, while intrinsic-modal alignment enhances semantic grounding and GenEval scores.
solution: A unified generative framework, UniAlignment, is developed around a single, shared Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone that avoids external Vision-Language Models (VLMs) during inference.

A dual-stream diffusion training strategy is employed, with continuous diffusion for image generation/manipulation/perception and discrete diffusion for text-based understanding, sharing the same DiT parameters.

Two semantic alignment mechanisms are introduced: Cross-Modal Semantic Alignment (contrastive learning) and Intrinsic-Modal Semantic Alignment (VLM-guided latent regularization), to enhance semantic grounding and instruction following.
A new rigorous benchmark, SemGen-Bench, is proposed with complex, compositional instructions for comprehensive evaluation of multimodal semantic consistency.
paper_summary.summary: UniAlignment introduces a unified multimodal generative framework utilizing a single Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone to perform image generation, understanding, manipulation, and perception tasks. The compact 2-billion-parameter model achieves top-2 performance across various benchmarks, including the new SemGen-Bench, and demonstrates significantly faster generation times (2s per image) by integrating novel cross-modal and intrinsic-modal semantic alignment strategies.

title: UniAlignment: Semantic Alignment for Unified Image Generation, Understanding, Manipulation and Perception

abstract: We present NextFlow, a unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer trained on 6 trillion interleaved text-image discrete tokens. By leveraging a unified vision representation within a unified autoregressive architecture, NextFlow natively activates multimodal understanding and generation capabilities, unlocking abilities of image editing, interleaved content and video generation. Motivated by the distinct nature of modalities - where text is strictly sequential and images are inherently hierarchical - we retain next-token prediction for text but adopt next-scale prediction for visual generation. This departs from traditional raster-scan methods, enabling the generation of 1024x1024 images in just 5 seconds - orders of magnitude faster than comparable AR models. We address the instabilities of multi-scale generation through a robust training recipe. Furthermore, we introduce a prefix-tuning strategy for reinforcement learning. Experiments demonstrate that NextFlow achieves state-of-the-art performance among unified models and rivals specialized diffusion baselines in visual quality.

keyInsights: A single autoregressive transformer can effectively unify multimodal understanding and generation, rivaling specialized diffusion models in visual quality and surpassing prior AR models in efficiency.

The next-scale prediction paradigm dramatically improves AR visual generation speed, reducing inference time for 1024x1024 images from minutes to seconds, making AR practical for interactive applications.

Semantic-rich dual-codebook tokenization, combined with a robust training odyssey (including progressive resolution and prefix-tuned RL), is crucial for activating advanced multimodal reasoning and high-fidelity generation.
originalProblem: Traditional multimodal AI systems separate large language models (LLMs) for understanding and diffusion models for high-fidelity image generation, creating architectural friction.
Pure autoregressive multimodal LLMs suffer from prohibitively slow high-resolution visual generation due to raster-scan token prediction and a semantic gap in their visual representations.
Hybrid AR-diffusion architectures, despite efforts, still rely on different representations, leading to re-encoding overheads and limiting deep multimodal integration.
results: NextFlow generates 1024x1024 images in 5 seconds (an order of magnitude faster than raster-scan AR models) with approximately 6x fewer FLOPs than MMDiT-based diffusion models, while achieving state-of-the-art prompt following (GenEval 0.84, DPG 88.32).

It scores highest on the ImgEdit benchmark (4.49 overall) and the new EditCanvas (8.04 overall) for image editing, demonstrating robust subject consistency (9.22 SC on OmniContext) and precise local modifications.

The model integrates Chain-of-Thought reasoning for visual synthesis, boosting the WISE score from 0.60 to 0.70, and exhibits strong in-context learning and coherent interleaved text-image generation.
solution: ByteDance developed NextFlow, a unified decoder-only autoregressive transformer, initialized from Qwen2.5-VL-7B and trained on 6 trillion interleaved text-image tokens.

It introduces a dual-codebook tokenizer for semantically rich and high-fidelity visual representations, coupled with a novel next-scale prediction paradigm for efficient hierarchical visual generation.

The model integrates a multiscale 3D RoPE positional encoding, learnable scale embeddings, and a scale-reweighting strategy, alongside a self-correction mechanism and an optional diffusion decoder for refinement.
paper_summary.summary: ByteDance's NextFlow unifies multimodal understanding and generation into a single autoregressive transformer. It achieves state-of-the-art performance in visual generation and understanding tasks, generating high-resolution images (1024x1024) in 5 seconds while demonstrating robust reasoning and editing capabilities across modalities.

title: NextFlow: Unified Sequential Modeling Activates Multimodal Understanding and Generation

abstract: Recent advances in diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in isolated computer vision tasks such as text-to-image generation, depth estimation, and optical flow. However, these models are often restricted by a ``single-task-single-model'' paradigm, severely limiting their generalizability and scalability in multi-task scenarios. Motivated by the cross-domain generalization ability of large language models, we propose a universal visual perception framework based on flow matching that can generate diverse visual representations across multiple tasks. Our approach formulates the process as a universal flow-matching problem from image patch tokens to task-specific representations rather than an independent generation or regression problem. By leveraging a strong self-supervised foundation model as the anchor and introducing a multi-scale, circular task embedding mechanism, our method learns a universal velocity field to bridge the gap between heterogeneous tasks, supporting efficient and flexible representation transfer. Extensive experiments on classification, detection, segmentation, depth estimation, and image-text retrieval demonstrate that our model achieves competitive performance in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings, outperforming prior generalist and several specialist models. Ablation studies further validate the robustness, scalability, and generalization of our framework. Our work marks a significant step towards general-purpose visual perception, providing a solid foundation for future research in universal vision modeling.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Formulating multi-vision perception as a flow-matching problem between a VFM's image patch tokens and task-specific representations allows for a unified and abstract approach.
Leveraging a powerful self-supervised VFM like DINOv2 provides robust, high-resolution initial tokens critical for diverse downstream tasks.
Adaptive conditioning through circular task embeddings and learnable scale embeddings enables the model to effectively handle the varied requirements of different vision tasks and scales.
originalProblem: The traditional 'single-task-single-model' paradigm in computer vision leads to scalability and generalizability challenges.
Existing generalist vision models often struggle to match the performance of specialist models on specific tasks or are limited to pixel-level generative objectives.
The inherent structural and functional heterogeneity of diverse vision tasks requires distinct model designs, hindering unification.
results: Achieved 81.5% Top-1 accuracy in zero-shot classification on ImageNet-1K, closely matching a supervised DeiT-B baseline, and improved to 82.1% with fine-tuning.
Demonstrated 39.2 mAP in zero-shot object detection on COCO, surpassing a DETR baseline with a ResNet-50 backbone (42.8 mAP) after fine-tuning.

Attained competitive zero-shot performance in semantic segmentation (37.9 PQ on ADE20K) and depth estimation (e.g., AbsRel 0.048 on KITTI), outperforming other generalist models like OneDiffusion by 8.0% in δ1 on KITTI.
solution: A universal flow-matching framework is proposed that learns a velocity field to transform image patch tokens from a frozen DINOv2 model into task-specific representations.
The framework utilizes a Diffusion Transformer (DiT-B) to model the transformation dynamics, conditioned on novel circular task embeddings and learnable scale embeddings.

Inference is performed by iteratively integrating the learned velocity field to evolve initial tokens towards target task representations, which are then processed by lightweight task-specific decoders.
paper_summary.summary: A universal flow-matching framework, named Visual Bridge, generates diverse visual perception representations by transforming image patch tokens from a Vision Foundation Model into task-specific outputs for various computer vision tasks. The framework achieves 81.5% Top-1 accuracy in zero-shot classification on ImageNet-1K and 39.2 mAP in zero-shot object detection on COCO, demonstrating competitive performance across generative, discriminative, and metric-based tasks.

title: Visual Bridge: Universal Visual Perception Representations Generating

abstract: Rectified flow models have become a de facto standard in image generation due to their stable sampling trajectories and high-fidelity outputs. Despite their strong generative capabilities, they face critical limitations in image editing tasks: inaccurate inversion processes for mapping real images back into the latent space, and gradient entanglement issues during editing often result in outputs that do not faithfully reflect the target prompt. Recent efforts have attempted to directly map source and target distributions via ODE-based approaches without inversion; however,these methods still yield suboptimal editing quality. In this work, we propose a flow decomposition-and-aggregation framework built upon an inversion-free formulation to address these limitations. Specifically, we semantically decompose the target prompt into multiple sub-prompts, compute an independent flow for each, and aggregate them to form a unified editing trajectory. While we empirically observe that decomposing the original flow enhances diversity in the target space, generating semantically aligned outputs still requires consistent guidance toward the full target prompt. To this end, we design a projection and soft-aggregation mechanism for flow, inspired by gradient conflict resolution in multi-task learning. This approach adaptively weights the sub-target velocity fields, suppressing semantic redundancy while emphasizing distinct directions, thereby preserving both diversity and consistency in the final edited output. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms existing zero-shot editing approaches in terms of semantic fidelity and attribute disentanglement. The code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Kempner Institute for the Study of Natural and Artificial Intelligence, Harvard University

keyInsights: Decomposing complex editing tasks into semantically distinct sub-flows, followed by intelligent aggregation, effectively mitigates gradient entanglement and improves control over individual attributes.

Latent Trajectory Projection (LTP) and Velocity Field Aggregation (VFA) can resolve conflicts between sub-flows and enhance flow diversity, leading to a better balance between image fidelity and editability.
The proposed framework is robust across different LLMs and backbone generative models, demonstrating the generalizability of flow decomposition for controllable image editing.
originalProblem: Existing text-to-image editing methods often rely on imprecise inversion processes, leading to semantic drift or visual distortions.
Complex target prompts cause 'gradient entanglement' or 'conflicting directions' in semantic space, making precise control of individual attributes difficult for generative models.
Prior inversion-free methods struggle with directional selectivity and disentanglement of multiple semantic attributes within complex prompts.
results: Outperformed the FlowEdit baseline on the PIE-Bench benchmark with an improved Structure Distance of 25.96 (vs. 27.24) and higher CLIP Similarity (26.96 vs. 26.83) using an SD3 model.
The fidelity-enhanced SplitFlow† variant achieved superior Structure Distance (14.55 vs. 18.17) and LPIPS (68.53 vs. 80.55) compared to FTEdit (SD3.5), with higher CLIP Similarity.

Qualitative evaluations show SplitFlow produces more precise attribute manipulations, maintains semantic consistency in complex scenarios, and reduces distortions, excelling in background preservation across diverse editing tasks.
solution: Decomposes complex target prompts into simpler, semantically localized sub-target prompts using a Large Language Model (LLM) during a progressive target approximation phase.

Employs a novel flow composition phase featuring Latent Trajectory Projection (LTP) for global semantic consistency and Velocity Field Aggregation (VFA) to combine sub-flows while resolving conflicts.
Integrates the decomposed and aggregated flow with a unified editing process guided by the original target prompt, leveraging Rectified Flow models like Stable Diffusion (SD3/SD3.5).
paper_summary.summary: SplitFlow introduces an inversion-free text-to-image editing framework that decomposes complex editing tasks into simpler sub-flows, guided by an LLM, and then intelligently aggregates these sub-flows to achieve precise and disentangled edits.
